parts.content: Define incomplete combustion.
parts.markscheme:
Combustion in a limited supply of oxygen, forming $\text{CO}$ or $\text{C}$ <marks>A1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Identify the oxygen condition
steps.content: The defining characteristic of incomplete combustion is the environment in which it occurs. Unlike complete combustion, which happens in excess oxygen, incomplete combustion occurs when there is a **limited supply of oxygen**.

steps.title: Identify the specific products
steps.content: Because there isn't enough oxygen to fully oxidize the fuel into carbon dioxide ($CO_2$), different products are formed. These include **carbon monoxide** ($CO$) and/or **carbon** ($C$), which appears as black soot.

steps.title: State the final definition
steps.content:
To earn the mark, your definition must combine both the condition and the products. 

**Incomplete combustion** is combustion that occurs in a **limited supply of oxygen**, resulting in the formation of **carbon monoxide** ($CO$) and/or **carbon** ($C$).

This concept is covered in **Topic R1.3.2: Incomplete combustion** of your syllabus.

highlights.text: Combustion in a limited supply of oxygen, forming CO or C

parts.content: Write a balanced chemical equation for the incomplete combustion of methane forming carbon monoxide.
parts.markscheme:
$2\text{CH}_4 + 3\text{O}_2 \to 2\text{CO} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$ <marks>A1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Identify the skeletal equation
steps.content:
First, we identify the reactants and the specific products mentioned in the question. Methane ($\text{CH}_4$) reacts with oxygen ($\text{O}_2$) to produce carbon monoxide ($\text{CO}$) and water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$).

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{O}_2 \to \text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

highlights.text: incomplete combustion of methane forming carbon monoxide

steps.title: Balance carbon atoms
steps.content:
Looking at our skeletal equation, we check the number of carbon atoms on both sides:
* **Left side:** 1 carbon atom (in $\text{CH}_4$)
* **Right side:** 1 carbon atom (in $\text{CO}$)

The carbon atoms are already balanced, so we don't need to change any coefficients yet.

steps.title: Balance hydrogen atoms
steps.content:
Next, we balance the hydrogen atoms:
* **Left side:** 4 hydrogen atoms (in $\text{CH}_4$)
* **Right side:** 2 hydrogen atoms (in $\text{H}_2\text{O}$)

To balance these, we need to place a coefficient of $2$ in front of the water molecule on the right side:

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{O}_2 \to \text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

steps.title: Balance oxygen atoms
steps.content:
Finally, we count the oxygen atoms on the right side to determine what is needed on the left:
* **Right side:** 1 oxygen (in $\text{CO}$) + 2 oxygens (in $2\text{H}_2\text{O}$) = 3 oxygen atoms total.
* **Left side:** Oxygen exists as diatomic molecules ($\text{O}_2$).

To get 3 atoms of oxygen from $\text{O}_2$, we need a coefficient of $\frac{3}{2}$:

$$\text{CH}_4 + \frac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 \to \text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

steps.title: Obtain integer coefficients
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, we must use the simplest whole-number coefficients. To remove the fraction, we multiply the entire equation by 2:

$$2(\text{CH}_4 + \frac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 \to \text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O})$$

This gives us the final balanced equation:

$$2\text{CH}_4 + 3\text{O}_2 \to 2\text{CO} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

steps.title: Review related concepts
steps.content: This process uses techniques covered in **Topic R2.1: Chemical equations and stoichiometry**. For more context on incomplete combustion specifically, you can review **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**.

steps.title: Verify final count
steps.content:
Checking our final result, we confirm the atom counts match on both sides:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Left C} &= 2 \\ \text{Right C} &= 2 \\ \text{Left H} &= 2 \times 4 = 8 \\ \text{Right H} &= 4 \times 2 = 8 \\ \text{Left O} &= 3 \times 2 = 6 \\ \text{Right O} &= (2 \times 1) + (4 \times 1) = 6 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: GDC: Balancing Equations
steps.content: While simple equations are best balanced manually, you can verify more complex stoichiometry using your calculator's simultaneous equation solver.

gdc.instructions: Use the 'Simultaneous Equation' solver in the Equation mode (typically [MENU] -> Equation).

ti84.instructions:
1. Go to [APPS] and select 'PLYMLT2'.
2. Select 'Simultaneous Equation Solver'.
3. Set equations to 3 and unknowns to 4.
4. Input the atom balance equations (e.g., for C: $1x_1 = 1x_3$). Note: Usually easier for larger systems.

desmos.instructions: Not applicable for direct equation balancing; manual calculation is preferred.

calculator.description: Solve for stoichiometric coefficients by setting up a system of equations for C, H, and O.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
Therefore, the balanced equation for the incomplete combustion of methane to carbon monoxide is:

$$2\text{CH}_4(\text{g}) + 3\text{O}_2(\text{g}) \to 2\text{CO}(\text{g}) + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{l})$$

steps.title: Identify the reaction components
steps.content:
Incomplete combustion occurs when there is limited oxygen. For methane ($\text{CH}_4$), the question specifies that the products are carbon monoxide ($\text{CO}$) and water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$). 

We start by assigning algebraic coefficients ($a, b, c, d$) to each substance in the unbalanced equation:

$$a\text{CH}_4 + b\text{O}_2 \to c\text{CO} + d\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

highlights.text: incomplete combustion of methane forming carbon monoxide

steps.title: Set up balance equations
steps.content:
According to the Law of Conservation of Mass, the number of atoms for each element must be the same on both sides of the equation. We can set up an equation for each element:

*   **Carbon (C):** $a = c$
*   **Hydrogen (H):** $4a = 2d$
*   **Oxygen (O):** $2b = c + d$

This is a fundamental concept in **Topic R2.1: Chemical equations and stoichiometry**.

steps.title: Solve the system
steps.content:
To solve these equations, we can assume a value for one coefficient. Let's set $a = 1$.

1.  From the **Carbon** equation: $c = a = 1$
2.  From the **Hydrogen** equation: $4(1) = 2d$, so $d = 2$
3.  From the **Oxygen** equation: $2b = c + d = 1 + 2 = 3$, so $b = 1.5$

Our coefficients are $a=1, b=1.5, c=1, d=2$.

ti84.instructions: Solve for d: 4/2. Solve for b: (1+2)/2.

calculator.description: Calculate coefficient d and b

steps.title: Convert to whole numbers
steps.content:
Chemical equations usually use the smallest whole-number coefficients. Since $b = 1.5$, we multiply all coefficients by $2$ to remove the fraction:

*   $a = 1 \times 2 = 2$
*   $b = 1.5 \times 2 = 3$
*   $c = 1 \times 2 = 2$
*   $d = 2 \times 2 = 4$

This gives us the final ratio for the balanced equation.

ti84.instructions: Multiply 1, 1.5, and 2 by 2.

calculator.description: Multiply coefficients by 2

steps.title: State final balanced equation
steps.content:
Substitute the whole-number coefficients back into the chemical equation:

$$2\text{CH}_4 + 3\text{O}_2 \to 2\text{CO} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

This reaction represents the incomplete combustion of methane as described in **Topic R1.3.2**.

parts.content: State another possible product of incomplete combustion besides carbon monoxide.
parts.markscheme:
Carbon (soot) / $\text{C}$ <marks>A1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Identify the reaction type
steps.content:
The question asks for a product of **incomplete combustion**. This occurs when there is an insufficient supply of oxygen to completely convert the fuel into carbon dioxide ($CO_2$) and water ($H_2O$). 

This concept is part of **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Analyze the visual observation
steps.content:
In the question stem, we are given a key observation: **"black soot forms on the glass"**. 

In chemistry, "soot" is the common name for the solid, black particles of unburnt material produced during the burning of hydrocarbons in limited oxygen.

highlights.text: black soot forms on the glass

steps.title: Identify the chemical element
steps.content:
By identifying the 'black soot' mentioned in the observation, we can determine the chemical product. Soot consists almost entirely of the element **carbon**. 

Therefore, besides carbon monoxide ($CO$) and water ($H_2O$), the other major product of incomplete combustion is solid carbon ($C$).

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The markscheme accepts either the name or the chemical symbol. 

Final Answer: **Carbon** or **$C$**

steps.title: Define incomplete combustion
steps.content: Incomplete combustion occurs when a fuel, such as the wax in a candle, reacts with a limited supply of oxygen. You can find more details on this in **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**, specifically section **R1.3.2 Incomplete combustion**.

steps.title: Recall the theoretical products
steps.content:
Theoretically, we recall that the incomplete combustion of hydrocarbons can produce several substances depending on the oxygen availability. These include:

- Carbon monoxide ($CO$)
- Carbon in the form of soot ($C$)
- Water ($H_2O$)

Since the question asks for a product *besides* carbon monoxide, we look for one of the others.

steps.title: Connect to the observations
steps.content: The question provides a clue by stating that "black soot forms on the glass." In a chemical context, soot is composed of unburned solid carbon particles.

highlights.text: black soot forms on the glass

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: According to the markscheme, the accepted answer is carbon (or soot). Although water ($H_2O$) is also produced, carbon is the specific product linked to the formation of soot mentioned in the prompt.

parts.content: Suggest why incomplete combustion is dangerous in enclosed spaces.
parts.markscheme:
Carbon monoxide is toxic and binds to hemoglobin, reducing oxygen transport <marks>M1</marks>
Dangerous in enclosed spaces due to lack of ventilation and buildup of toxic gases <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Identify the toxic product
steps.content:
When a fuel undergoes incomplete combustion (usually due to a limited oxygen supply), it produces **carbon monoxide** ($CO$) instead of carbon dioxide ($CO_2$). 

This process is covered in **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**, specifically section **R1.3.2 Incomplete combustion**.

steps.title: Explain the toxicological mechanism
steps.content:
Carbon monoxide is dangerous because it is a toxic gas that binds preferentially to **hemoglobin** in the blood. 

It has a much higher affinity for hemoglobin than oxygen ($O_2$) does. When $CO$ binds to hemoglobin, it forms carboxyhemoglobin, which effectively blocks the transport of oxygen to the body's tissues and organs. This leads to internal suffocation (hypoxia).

steps.title: Address the enclosed space
steps.content:
The danger is significantly higher in **enclosed spaces** because there is a lack of ventilation. Without fresh air flow, the concentration of the toxic carbon monoxide gas builds up rapidly. 

Because $CO$ is colorless and odorless, people in the room may not realize a toxic buildup is occurring until they experience symptoms like headaches or dizziness.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks for this question, you must mention both the biological impact and the environmental constraint:

1.  **M1**: State that carbon monoxide is toxic and binds to hemoglobin, which reduces oxygen transport in the blood.
2.  **A1**: Explain that in enclosed spaces, the lack of ventilation leads to a dangerous buildup of these toxic gases.

steps.title: Identify products of incomplete combustion
steps.content:
Incomplete combustion occurs when there is a limited supply of oxygen. This process produces several dangerous byproducts instead of just carbon dioxide and water. For a candle (a hydrocarbon), the reaction is:

$$\text{Hydrocarbon} + \text{limited } O_2(g) \rightarrow C(s) \text{ (soot)} + CO(g) + H_2O(g)$$

The physical appearance of **soot** (solid carbon) is a primary indicator that these hazardous conditions are present.

steps.title: Hazards of particulate matter
steps.content:
The "black soot" mentioned in the question is **particulate matter**. These tiny solid carbon particles are hazardous because:
1. They act as a physical irritant to the respiratory system when inhaled.
2. They can penetrate deep into the lungs, potentially causing long-term damage.
3. Their presence indicates a lack of ventilation, signaling that other invisible toxic gases are likely building up.

steps.title: Toxicity of carbon monoxide
steps.content: While soot is the visible danger, incomplete combustion also produces **carbon monoxide ($CO$)**. This gas is extremely dangerous because it is toxic and binds to **hemoglobin** in the blood much more strongly than oxygen does. This significantly **reduces oxygen transport** to vital organs.

highlights.text: Carbon monoxide is toxic and binds to hemoglobin, reducing oxygen transport

steps.title: The danger of enclosed spaces
steps.content: In an enclosed space, such as the jar in the experiment or a room without windows, there is a **lack of ventilation**. This causes a rapid **buildup** of both soot and toxic gases. Because oxygen is not being replenished, the combustion becomes increasingly incomplete, making the environment lethal very quickly.

highlights.text: Dangerous in enclosed spaces due to lack of ventilation and buildup of toxic gases

steps.title: Identify the toxic byproduct
steps.content:
When combustion is incomplete due to a limited oxygen supply, it doesn't just produce carbon dioxide ($CO_2$); it also produces **carbon monoxide ($CO$)**. 

This concept is a key part of **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**. Unlike $CO_2$, carbon monoxide is a highly toxic, colorless, and odorless gas, making it extremely difficult to detect without specialized equipment.

steps.title: Explain physiological impact
steps.content:
Carbon monoxide is dangerous because it has a much higher affinity for **hemoglobin** in your red blood cells than oxygen does. 

When inhaled, $CO$ binds strongly to hemoglobin, effectively "blocking" the sites where oxygen should attach. This significantly **reduces the blood's ability to transport oxygen** to vital organs like the brain and heart.

steps.title: Analyze the enclosed space
steps.content:
The specific danger of **enclosed spaces** comes down to the **Accumulation and Ventilation Approach**. 

In an open area, $CO$ can dissipate. However, in a closed room or jar, there is a **lack of ventilation**. This means:
1. Fresh oxygen cannot enter to replace what is consumed.
2. Toxic gases like $CO$ cannot escape, leading to a rapid **buildup** of lethal concentrations.

Because the space is sealed, the concentration of $CO$ increases over time while breathable oxygen decreases, creating a deadly environment.

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks, your answer should clearly link the chemical byproduct to the physical environment:

*   **M1:** Carbon monoxide is toxic because it binds to hemoglobin, reducing oxygen transport in the body.
*   **A1:** In enclosed spaces, the lack of ventilation causes these toxic gases to build up to dangerous levels.

parts.content: Compare the energy released in complete and incomplete combustion of the same fuel.
parts.markscheme:
Incomplete combustion releases less energy than complete combustion <marks>A1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Identify the reaction types
steps.content: In the scenario described, the presence of soot and a limited oxygen supply indicates that **incomplete combustion** is occurring. We are asked to compare the energy output of this process to **complete combustion** for the same fuel.

steps.title: Compare energy release
steps.content:
This question tests your understanding of **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**. 

In chemical terms, complete combustion is more efficient because it fully oxidizes the carbon in the fuel to $CO_2$, releasing the maximum amount of energy possible. Incomplete combustion leaves some of that potential energy behind in products like carbon (soot) or carbon monoxide ($CO$). 

Therefore, for the same amount of fuel:

$$\text{Energy released (complete)} > \text{Energy released (incomplete)}$$

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content: According to the markscheme, the key point is to state that incomplete combustion releases less energy than complete combustion.

highlights.text: Incomplete combustion releases less energy than complete combustion

steps.title: Identify the combustion types
steps.content:
To compare the energy released, we first look at the products formed in each process. The prompt mentions that "black soot forms," which is a clear indicator that the fuel is undergoing **incomplete combustion** due to the "limited oxygen."

In **complete combustion**, there is enough oxygen to convert all the carbon in the fuel into carbon dioxide ($CO_2$).

steps.title: Analyze oxidation extent
steps.content:
The amount of energy released depends on the **extent of oxidation** of the carbon atoms:

*   **Complete combustion:** Carbon is fully oxidized to $CO_2$. This process maximizes the number of strong bonds formed with oxygen, releasing the maximum amount of energy.
*   **Incomplete combustion:** Carbon is only partially oxidized, forming carbon monoxide ($CO$) or even solid carbon (soot, $C$). 

Because the carbon in incomplete combustion does not reach its maximum oxidation state (it doesn't "finish" the reaction to $CO_2$), it cannot release its full potential energy.

steps.title: Compare energy release
steps.content:
Since incomplete combustion results in products that are less oxidized than $CO_2$, the chemical system does not drop to its lowest possible energy state. 

Therefore, **incomplete combustion releases less energy** than the complete combustion of the same amount of fuel.

parts.content: Write the balanced overall equation for the methanol fuel cell.
parts.markscheme:
- Write the balanced overall equation: $\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \frac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the overall reaction
steps.content:
In electrochemical fuel cells, the overall reaction is identical to the **complete combustion** of the fuel. For a methanol fuel cell, methanol ($ \text{CH}_3\text{OH} $) reacts with oxygen ($ \text{O}_2 $) to produce carbon dioxide ($ \text{CO}_2 $) and water ($ \text{H}_2\text{O} $).

This concept is a key part of **Reactivity 1.3: Energy from fuels**, where we learn that fuel cells provide a path for these combustion reactants to produce electricity directly.

steps.title: Write the skeletal equation
steps.content:
First, write down the reactants and products involved in the combustion process:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

steps.title: Balance Carbon and Hydrogen
steps.content:
We balance the atoms in the following order:

1.  **Carbon**: There is $1$ Carbon atom in $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$, so we need $1$ molecule of $\text{CO}_2$ on the right side.
2.  **Hydrogen**: There are $4$ Hydrogen atoms in total in $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$ (3 in the methyl group + 1 in the hydroxyl group). To balance this, we need $2$ molecules of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ to get $4$ Hydrogen atoms on the right side ($2 \times 2 = 4$).

Our equation now looks like this:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

steps.title: Balance Oxygen atoms
steps.content:
Count the Oxygen atoms to find the coefficient for $\text{O}_2$:

*   **Products side**: We have $(1 \times 2 \text{ in } \text{CO}_2) + (2 \times 1 \text{ in } \text{H}_2\text{O}) = 4$ Oxygen atoms.
*   **Reactants side**: Methanol already contains **1** Oxygen atom. 

We need $3$ more Oxygen atoms from the $\text{O}_2$ gas. Since each oxygen molecule has $2$ atoms, we need $\frac{3}{2}$ (or $1.5$) molecules of $\text{O}_2$.

gdc.instructions: Input (4 - 1) / 2 to find the required coefficient.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the oxygen in the fuel from the total needed in the products, then divide by 2.

calculator.description: Calculate the coefficient for O2 gas.

steps.title: State the final equation
steps.content:
The balanced overall equation for the methanol fuel cell is:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \frac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

In IB Chemistry, using fractional coefficients for $\text{O}_2$ is common and perfectly correct. Alternatively, you could multiply the entire equation by $2$ to use whole numbers:

$$2\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

steps.title: Anode oxidation half-equation
steps.content:
In a fuel cell, oxidation occurs at the anode. For a methanol fuel cell, methanol ($\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$) is oxidized to carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$). To balance this under acidic conditions, we follow the standard redox balancing steps:

1. **Balance atoms other than H and O**: The carbons are already balanced (1 on each side).
2. **Balance Oxygen**: Add one $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ molecule to the left to balance the oxygens.
3. **Balance Hydrogen**: Add $6\text{H}^+$ ions to the right side.
4. **Balance Charge**: Add $6e^-$ to the right side to balance the charge.

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH(l)} + \text{H}_2\text{O(l)} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2\text{(g)} + 6\text{H}^+\text{(aq)} + 6e^-$$

steps.title: Cathode reduction half-equation
steps.content:
Reduction occurs at the cathode, where oxygen ($\text{O}_2$) from the air is reduced to water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$):

1. **Balance Oxygen**: Add a coefficient of 2 to $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ to balance the two oxygen atoms from $\text{O}_2$.
2. **Balance Hydrogen**: Add $4\text{H}^+$ ions to the left side.
3. **Balance Charge**: Add $4e^-$ to the left side.

$$\text{O}_2\text{(g)} + 4\text{H}^+\text{(aq)} + 4e^- \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O(l)}$$

steps.title: Equalizing electrons
steps.content:
To combine the half-equations, the number of electrons lost must equal the number of electrons gained. The lowest common multiple of 6 (from methanol) and 4 (from oxygen) is 12.

* Multiply the oxidation half-equation by 2:
$$2\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 12\text{H}^+ + 12e^-$$

* Multiply the reduction half-equation by 3:
$$3\text{O}_2 + 12\text{H}^+ + 12e^- \rightarrow 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

gdc.instructions: Find the least common multiple of 6 and 4.

ti84.instructions: Use the LCM function if available or manually check multiples: 6*2=12, 4*3=12.

calculator.description: Calculate the LCM of the electron counts to determine the multiplication factors.

steps.title: Derive the overall equation
steps.content:
Now, sum the two equations and cancel out species that appear on both sides ($12e^-$, $12\text{H}^+$, and $2\text{H}_2\text{O}$):

$$2\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

For a standard chemical equation, we often divide by 2 to show the reaction per mole of methanol:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH(l)} + \frac{3}{2}\text{O}_2\text{(g)} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2\text{(g)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O(l)}$$

steps.title: Identify reactants and products
steps.content:
To deduce the half-equation for the **oxidation** of methanol ($\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$), we first identify the starting material and the final product. In a fuel cell, carbon-based fuels are oxidized to carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$). This process involves electron transfer, which is a core concept in **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**.

We start with the skeletal equation:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$$

highlights.text: oxidation of methanol at the anode

steps.title: Balance carbon atoms
steps.content:
Following the **Systematic Balancing Method**, we first balance atoms other than oxygen and hydrogen. In this case, that is carbon.

Since there is 1 carbon atom in methanol and 1 carbon atom in carbon dioxide, the carbons are already balanced:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$$

steps.title: Balance oxygen with water
steps.content:
Next, we balance the oxygen atoms by adding water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$) molecules to the side that is deficient in oxygen.

*   **Left side:** 1 oxygen atom (in $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$)
*   **Right side:** 2 oxygen atoms (in $\text{CO}_2$)

We need to add 1 water molecule to the left side:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$$

steps.title: Balance hydrogen with protons
steps.content:
Now we balance the hydrogen atoms by adding hydrogen ions ($\text{H}^+$) to the opposite side. 

Let's count the hydrogens on the left:
*   4 from methanol ($\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$)
*   2 from water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$)
*   Total = $4 + 2 = 6$

We add $6\text{H}^+$ to the right side:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}^+$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 4 + 2 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Summing hydrogen atoms on the reactant side.

steps.title: Balance charge with electrons
steps.content:
The final step in the systematic method is to balance the overall charge by adding electrons ($\text{e}^-$).

*   **Left side charge:** 0 (neutral species)
*   **Right side charge:** $+6$ (from the $6\text{H}^+$)

To make both sides equal zero, we must add 6 electrons to the right side. This confirms it is an **oxidation** reaction, as electrons are being lost (produced).

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}^+ + 6\text{e}^-$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 0 - 6 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Determining the number of electrons needed to neutralize a +6 charge.

steps.title: Identify reactants and products
steps.content:
In a direct methanol fuel cell, oxidation occurs at the anode. This involves converting methanol ($\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$) into carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$). Our first task is to set up the skeleton of the half-equation.

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$$

highlights.text: oxidation of methanol at the anode

steps.title: Calculate carbon oxidation states
steps.content:
Using the rules from **Topic S3.1.6: Oxidation states**, we determine the oxidation state (OS) of carbon in both species:

1. **In $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$**: 
   - $\text{H}$ is $+1$ and $\text{O}$ is $-2$.
   - $\text{OS(C)} + 4(+1) + (-2) = 0$
   - $\text{OS(C)} + 2 = 0 \Rightarrow \text{OS(C)} = -2$

2. **In $\text{CO}_2$**: 
   - $\text{O}$ is $-2$.
   - $\text{OS(C)} + 2(-2) = 0 \Rightarrow \text{OS(C)} = +4$

gdc.instructions: Subtract the initial oxidation state from the final oxidation state.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the initial oxidation state from the final oxidation state.

desmos.instructions: Subtract the initial oxidation state from the final oxidation state.

calculator.description: Calculate the change in oxidation state for Carbon.

steps.title: Determine electrons transferred
steps.content:
The oxidation state of carbon increases from $-2$ to $+4$. This represents a loss of $6$ electrons per carbon atom. We add these electrons to the product side to represent oxidation:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 6\text{e}^-$$

steps.title: Balance oxygen using water
steps.content:
Now we look at the oxygen atoms. 
- The reactant side has $1$ oxygen (in $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$).
- The product side has $2$ oxygens (in $\text{CO}_2$).

To balance them, we add one molecule of water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$) to the left side:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 6\text{e}^-$$

steps.title: Balance hydrogen using protons
steps.content:
Finally, we balance the hydrogen atoms. 
- The left side now has $4$ H from methanol and $2$ H from water, totaling $6$ H atoms.
- We add $6\text{H}^+$ to the right side to balance them:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}^+ + 6\text{e}^-$$

steps.title: Final verification
steps.content:
Let's double-check the balance:
- **Atoms**: $\text{C} (1=1)$, $\text{O} (2=2)$, $\text{H} (6=6)$.
- **Charge**: The left side is neutral ($0$). The right side is $+6$ (from $6\text{H}^+$) and $-6$ (from $6\text{e}^-$), which equals $0$.

This half-equation is correctly balanced and earns full marks by identifying all required species and the correct stoichiometry.

methods.label: Direct Recall of Fuel Cell Principles

steps.title: Identify electrode function
steps.content: In any fuel cell, including a direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC), the **cathode** is the electrode where reduction occurs. This is a fundamental concept in **Topic R3.2.5: Electrochemical cells**.

steps.title: Recall reduction at cathode
steps.content:
In standard fuel cell operation, oxygen ($O_2$) from the air is the oxidant. It undergoes reduction at the cathode by reacting with hydrogen ions ($H^+$) that have migrated through the electrolyte from the anode. 

The half-equation for this process is:

$$O_2(g) + 4H^+(aq) + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O(l)$$ 

This shows that oxygen gas is converted into liquid water.

steps.title: State the product
steps.content:
Based on the standard reduction reaction, the product formed at the cathode is water. This is consistent across most common fuel cells (hydrogen or methanol-based).

Final Answer: **Water** or **$\text{H}_2\text{O}$**

steps.title: Identify the cathode process
steps.content:
In a fuel cell, the cathode is the electrode where **reduction** occurs. In a direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC), oxygen gas ($O_2$) from the air is the reactant that undergoes reduction at the cathode. To find the product, we need to look at the reduction half-equation in the acidic environment typical of these cells.

This concept is covered in **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions** of your syllabus.

steps.title: Balance the reduction half-equation
steps.content:
We follow the standard IB method for balancing redox half-equations in acidic conditions:

1. **Start with the reactant:** Oxygen gas ($O_2$).
2. **Balance Oxygen atoms:** We add water molecules ($H_2O$) to the product side.
   $$O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$$
3. **Balance Hydrogen atoms:** Since the cell operates in an acidic environment, we add hydrogen ions ($H^+$) to the reactant side.
   $$O_2 + 4H^+ \rightarrow 2H_2O$$
4. **Balance the charge:** We add electrons ($e^-$) to the more positive side to make the total charge on both sides equal (zero in this case).
   $$O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O$$

steps.title: Identify the final product
steps.content:
By looking at the completed reduction half-equation, we can see that the only chemical species formed as a result of the reduction of oxygen is water ($H_2O$).

$$O_2(g) + 4H^+(aq) + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O(l)$$ 

Therefore, the product formed at the cathode is water.

steps.title: Understand the fuel cell process
steps.content:
A fuel cell operates by converting chemical energy into electrical energy through a redox reaction. The overall chemical change in a methanol fuel cell is identical to the complete combustion of methanol. 

This concept is part of **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**, specifically focusing on how fuel cells use oxidation and reduction to generate power.

steps.title: Determine the overall reaction
steps.content:
To find the products, we look at the balanced equation for the complete combustion of methanol ($CH_3OH$) in oxygen ($O_2$):

$$2CH_3OH(l) + 3O_2(g) \rightarrow 2CO_2(g) + 4H_2O(l)$$

The two main products of this process are carbon dioxide and water.

steps.title: Deduce the cathode product
steps.content:
In a fuel cell:
1. The **fuel** (methanol) is oxidized at the **anode**. Since methanol contains carbon, it is converted into $CO_2$ at the anode.
2. The **oxidant** (oxygen) is reduced at the **cathode**.

Looking at our overall equation, if $CO_2$ is produced from the fuel at the anode, then the remaining product, **water**, must be formed at the cathode where oxygen reacts with hydrogen ions ($H^+$) and electrons ($e^-$).

steps.title: State the final product
steps.content:
Based on the overall reaction deduction, the product formed at the cathode is water.

Final Answer: **Water** (or $H_2O$)

steps.title: Analyze the oxidation products
steps.content: To evaluate the environmental impact, we need to look at the chemical products of the redox reactions occurring in both types of fuel cells. In a fuel cell, the fuel is oxidized at the anode to produce electricity and specific byproducts.

steps.title: Evaluate methanol oxidation
steps.content:
In a direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC), methanol ($\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$) reacts with oxygen. The overall cell reaction is:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH(l)} + \frac{3}{2}\text{O}_2\text{(g)} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2\text{(g)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O(l)}$$

Notice that because methanol contains carbon, its oxidation necessarily produces **carbon dioxide** ($\text{CO}_2$).

steps.title: Compare with hydrogen
steps.content:
In contrast, a hydrogen fuel cell uses the following reaction:

$$\text{H}_2\text{(g)} + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2\text{(g)} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O(l)}$$

Hydrogen oxidation produces only water, which has no negative environmental impact at the point of use.

steps.title: Identify the environmental disadvantage
steps.content:
Since carbon dioxide is a **greenhouse gas** that contributes to global warming, its production is a significant environmental disadvantage of using methanol compared to hydrogen.

According to the markscheme, any of the following are acceptable, but our environmental analysis specifically identifies:
- **Produces carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$)**

steps.title: Identify chemical properties
steps.content: To evaluate the disadvantages of using methanol ($CH_3OH$) compared to hydrogen ($H_2$), we first look at their chemical nature. While both are used as fuels in electrochemical cells, they have very different biological effects on the human body.

highlights.text: Suggest one disadvantage of methanol compared to hydrogen in fuel cell applications.

steps.title: Analyze toxicity risks
steps.content:
Using a **Safety and Toxicity Analysis**, we identify that methanol is a highly toxic substance. If ingested, inhaled, or absorbed through the skin, it can cause severe health issues, including permanent blindness or death, due to its metabolism into formaldehdye and formic acid in the body.

In contrast, hydrogen gas ($H_2$) is non-toxic. While it is highly flammable, it does not pose the same chemical poisoning risk to humans that methanol does during handling or accidental exposure.

steps.title: State the disadvantage
steps.content:
Based on this safety profile, a significant disadvantage of methanol is its **toxicity**. This makes it more dangerous to handle and transport compared to hydrogen in consumer or industrial fuel cell applications.

According to the markscheme, other acceptable answers include its lower energy density or the production of $CO_2$, but toxicity is a primary safety concern.

steps.title: Define energy density
steps.content:
To compare these two fuels using the **Energy Density Comparison** approach, we first need to understand what energy density is. In IB Chemistry, **energy density** refers to the amount of energy released per unit mass of fuel (usually measured in $kJ\,g^{-1}$ or $MJ\,kg^{-1}$).

This is a key concept found in **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**.

steps.title: Compare molar masses
steps.content:
Let's look at the mass of one mole of each fuel:
- **Hydrogen ($H_2$):** has a very small molar mass of approximately $2.02\,g\,mol^{-1}$.
- **Methanol ($CH_3OH$):** has a much larger molar mass of approximately $32.05\,g\,mol^{-1}$.

Because methanol molecules contain carbon and oxygen atoms, they are significantly heavier than simple hydrogen molecules.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the molar mass of methanol.

ti84.instructions: Enter the sum of the atomic masses.

desmos.instructions: Enter the molar mass expression for methanol.

calculator.description: Calculate the molar mass of methanol (CH3OH) using atomic masses from the periodic table (C=12.01, H=1.01, O=16.00).

steps.title: Evaluate energy per mass
steps.content:
Even though methanol releases more energy *per mole* ($726\,kJ\,mol^{-1}$) than hydrogen does ($286\,kJ\,mol^{-1}$), the mass of methanol required to produce that energy is much higher.

$$\text{Energy Density} = \frac{\text{Enthalpy of Combustion}}{\text{Molar Mass}}$$

If we calculate this for both:
- **Hydrogen:** $\approx 142\,kJ\,g^{-1}$
- **Methanol:** $\approx 22.7\,kJ\,g^{-1}$

This shows that hydrogen is a much more "concentrated" source of energy by mass.

ti84.instructions: Divide the enthalpy of combustion by the molar mass for both fuels.

desmos.instructions: Calculate energy density of hydrogen vs methanol.

calculator.description: Compare energy density (kJ/g) by dividing enthalpy of combustion by molar mass.

steps.title: State the disadvantage
steps.content: Based on our comparison, the primary disadvantage of methanol is its **lower energy density**. This means you would need to carry a much larger mass of methanol to produce the same amount of energy as a smaller mass of hydrogen, which is a significant drawback for portable or transport applications.

parts.content: Write a balanced chemical equation for the complete combustion of propane ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$), a component of natural gas.
parts.markscheme:
Balanced equation for the complete combustion:
$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\text{O}_2 \to 3\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Write the skeleton equation
steps.content:
For the complete combustion of a hydrocarbon like propane, the reactants are the fuel and oxygen ($O_2$), and the products are always carbon dioxide ($CO_2$) and water ($H_2O$). 

We start by writing the unbalanced skeleton equation:

$$C_3H_8 + O_2 \to CO_2 + H_2O$$

steps.title: Balance Carbon atoms
steps.content:
Following the systematic approach, we balance Carbon ($C$) first. There are 3 carbon atoms in one molecule of propane ($C_3H_8$). 

To have 3 carbons on the product side, we must place a coefficient of 3 in front of $CO_2$:

$$C_3H_8 + O_2 \to 3CO_2 + H_2O$$

steps.title: Balance Hydrogen atoms
steps.content:
Next, we balance Hydrogen ($H$). Propane has 8 hydrogen atoms. 

On the product side, each water molecule ($H_2O$) contains 2 hydrogen atoms. To get 8 hydrogens, we need 4 water molecules ($8 \div 2 = 4$):

$$C_3H_8 + O_2 \to 3CO_2 + 4H_2O$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the reactant hydrogen count (8) by the product hydrogen count per molecule (2).

ti84.instructions: Divide the number of hydrogen atoms in propane by the number of hydrogen atoms in one water molecule.

calculator.description: Calculate the coefficient for water to balance hydrogen atoms.

steps.title: Balance Oxygen atoms
steps.content:
Finally, we balance Oxygen ($O$). First, count the total oxygen atoms on the product side:
- From $3CO_2$: $3 \times 2 = 6$ oxygen atoms
- From $4H_2O$: $4 \times 1 = 4$ oxygen atoms
- Total: $6 + 4 = 10$ oxygen atoms

To balance this on the reactant side, we need 10 oxygen atoms. Since oxygen exists as $O_2$ molecules, we need 5 molecules ($10 \div 2 = 5$):

$$C_3H_8 + 5O_2 \to 3CO_2 + 4H_2O$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate (3*2 + 4*1) and then divide the result by 2.

ti84.instructions: Sum the oxygen atoms in the products, then divide by 2.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the total oxygen atoms in the products divided by 2.

calculator.description: Calculate total oxygen atoms needed and the resulting coefficient for O2.

steps.title: Final balanced equation
steps.content:
The atoms are now balanced on both sides: 3 C, 8 H, and 10 O. This equation aligns with the requirements for **Reactivity 2.1: Chemical equations and stoichiometry**.

Final Answer:
$$C_3H_8 + 5O_2 \to 3CO_2 + 4H_2O$$

steps.title: Identify the hydrocarbon values
steps.content:
To balance the combustion of propane using the general formula approach, we first identify the number of carbon ($x$) and hydrogen ($y$) atoms in the molecule. This falls under the study of stoichiometry in **Topic R2.1**.

For propane, $C_3H_8$:
$$x = 3$$
$$y = 8$$

steps.title: Calculate oxygen coefficient
steps.content:
The general formula for the complete combustion of any hydrocarbon ($C_xH_y$) is:

$$C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)O_2 \to xCO_2 + \frac{y}{2}H_2O$$

First, let's calculate the coefficient for oxygen ($O_2$):

$$\text{Coefficient of } O_2 = x + \frac{y}{4} = 3 + \frac{8}{4}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 3 + 8 / 4 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the oxygen coefficient.

steps.title: Determine product coefficients
steps.content:
Now we determine the coefficients for the products, carbon dioxide and water, using our $x$ and $y$ values:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Coefficient of } CO_2 &= x = 3 \\ \text{Coefficient of } H_2O &= \frac{y}{2} = \frac{8}{2} = 4 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Write the final equation
steps.content:
Substitute all the calculated coefficients back into the equation. In IB exams, marks are awarded for having the correct species and the correct balancing numbers.

$$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\text{O}_2 \to 3\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

parts.content:
The energy released per gram of fuel is:
Coal = $30\ \text{kJ}\ \text{g}^{-1}$; Methane = $55\ \text{kJ}\ \text{g}^{-1}$

Compare the energy output of these fuels and suggest an advantage of methane based on this data.
parts.markscheme:
- State that methane releases more energy per gram than coal <marks>A1</marks>
- Suggest that less fuel is needed to produce the same amount of energy <marks>R1</marks>

steps.title: Analyze provided energy data
steps.content:
To compare these two fossil fuels, we first look at the values given for their specific energy (energy released per unit mass). This topic is covered in **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**. 

The question provides the following values:
- Coal: $30\ \text{kJ}\ \text{g}^{-1}$
- Methane: $55\ \text{kJ}\ \text{g}^{-1}$

steps.title: Compare numerical outputs
steps.content:
By comparing the two values directly, we can see that methane has a significantly higher energy output for every gram burned compared to coal. 

$$\text{Methane } (55\ \text{kJ}\ \text{g}^{-1}) > \text{Coal } (30\ \text{kJ}\ \text{g}^{-1})$$

This observation earns the first mark ($A1$) for stating that methane releases more energy per gram than coal.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the difference between the energy density of methane and coal.

ti84.instructions: Enter the subtraction to find the difference in energy output per gram.

desmos.instructions: Subtract the coal energy value from the methane energy value.

calculator.description: Verify the difference in energy output between methane and coal.

steps.title: Deduce the mass advantage
steps.content:
Since methane provides more energy per gram, we can deduce a practical advantage. If a power plant needs to generate a specific, fixed amount of energy, it will require a **smaller mass** of methane than coal to reach that target. 

This is a major logistical advantage because less fuel needs to be transported and stored for the same energy yield. This deduction earns the second mark ($R1$).

gdc.instructions: Find the ratio of energy outputs.

ti84.instructions: Divide the energy of methane by coal to see the efficiency factor.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of mass required (Coal/Methane) to produce the same energy.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
In summary, based on the data provided:
- **Comparison:** Methane releases more energy per gram than coal.
- **Advantage:** Less mass of fuel (methane) is required to produce a given amount of energy.

steps.title: Identify given energy values
steps.content:
To compare the fuels, we first look at the energy released per unit mass (specific energy) provided in the question. These values tell us how much energy is obtained from every 1 gram of the fuel burned.

- Specific energy of Coal: $30\ \text{kJ}\ \text{g}^{-1}$
- Specific energy of Methane: $55\ \text{kJ}\ \text{g}^{-1}$

steps.title: Calculate the energy ratio
steps.content:
Using a **Quantitative Ratio Analysis**, we can determine exactly how much more energy methane provides compared to coal by dividing the energy value of methane by that of coal:

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{55\ \text{kJ}\ \text{g}^{-1}}{30\ \text{kJ}\ \text{g}^{-1}} \approx 1.83$$

This calculation shows that methane is approximately $1.8$ times more energy-dense by mass than coal.

gdc.instructions: In the main calculation menu, perform the division 55 / 30.

ti84.instructions: Enter 55 divided by 30 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the division into the expression box.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of energy release per gram for methane vs coal.

steps.title: Evaluate the advantage
steps.content:
Since methane releases significantly more energy per gram ($1.8$ times more), it is a more efficient fuel source. 

A key advantage of this higher energy density is that **less mass of fuel is required** to produce the same total amount of energy. This reduces costs and logistical efforts associated with fuel transport and storage.

In an IB exam, you earn marks for:
1. Stating that methane releases more energy per gram than coal.
2. Explaining that this means less fuel is needed for the same energy output.

parts.content: Describe two environmental advantages of using natural gas instead of coal.
parts.markscheme:
- State that natural gas produces less $\text{CO}_2$ per unit of energy released <marks>A1</marks>
- Identify that natural gas emits fewer pollutants such as sulfur dioxide, particulates, or ash <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the fuel types
steps.content: To understand the environmental advantages, we first look at what these fuels are made of. Coal is primarily carbon ($C$) with many impurities, while natural gas is mostly methane ($CH_4$). This difference in chemical composition directly affects the products released during combustion, which is a key concept in **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**.

steps.title: Compare carbon dioxide emissions
steps.content:
The first major advantage relates to greenhouse gas emissions. Because natural gas ($CH_4$) has a higher hydrogen-to-carbon ratio compared to coal, it produces **less carbon dioxide** ($CO_2$) for every joule of energy released.

$$\text{Natural Gas: } CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O + \text{energy}$$
$$\text{Coal: } C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + \text{energy}$$

Since $CO_2$ is a major greenhouse gas contributing to global warming, producing less of it per unit of energy is a significant environmental benefit.

highlights.text: natural gas produces less CO2 per unit of energy released

steps.title: Identify specific pollutants
steps.content:
The second advantage involves 'cleaner' combustion. Coal contains significant amounts of sulfur and non-combustible minerals. When coal burns, it releases:
- **Sulfur dioxide** ($SO_2$), which leads to acid rain.
- **Particulates** (soot) and **ash**, which contribute to respiratory issues and smog.

Natural gas is a gas that contains very few of these impurities, meaning it emits significantly fewer of these harmful pollutants during combustion.

highlights.text: natural gas emits fewer pollutants such as sulfur dioxide, particulates, or ash

steps.title: Summarize for marks
steps.content:
To earn the 2 marks available, you should clearly state these two distinct points:
1. Natural gas produces less $CO_2$ per unit of energy released (reducing the impact on climate change).
2. Natural gas produces fewer additional pollutants like sulfur dioxide (which causes acid rain) or solid particulates/ash.

parts.content: Suggest one disadvantage of relying heavily on natural gas as an energy source.
parts.markscheme: Suggest one disadvantage such as its non-renewable nature, contribution to greenhouse gas emissions (CO$_2$), or the concern of methane leakage <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the combustion product
steps.content:
Natural gas consists primarily of methane ($CH_4$). Like all fossil fuels, when natural gas is burned (combusted) to generate electricity or heat, it undergoes a chemical reaction with oxygen. 

This process is covered in **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**. The complete combustion of methane is represented by the equation:

$$CH_4(g) + 2O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g) + 2H_2O(l)$$ 

Even though natural gas produces less carbon dioxide per joule of energy compared to coal, it still releases significant amounts of $CO_2$ into the atmosphere.

steps.title: Link to environmental impact
steps.content:
The primary disadvantage identified through this approach is that carbon dioxide ($CO_2$) is a **greenhouse gas**. 

When $CO_2$ accumulates in the atmosphere, it traps heat through the enhanced greenhouse effect. This process is a major driver of global warming and subsequent climate change. Therefore, relying heavily on natural gas continues to contribute to the global carbon footprint and environmental degradation.

highlights.text: disadvantage of relying heavily on natural gas

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you simply need to state that natural gas combustion releases $CO_2$, which contributes to global warming. 

**Final Answer:** 
A disadvantage of natural gas is its contribution to greenhouse gas emissions (specifically $CO_2$), which leads to global warming.

steps.title: Classify the fuel type
steps.content: Natural gas is classified as a **fossil fuel**, similar to coal and oil. These fuels are covered in **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**, specifically under the subtopic of fossil fuels.

steps.title: Understand non-renewable nature
steps.content: A resource is considered **non-renewable** if it is a finite resource that cannot be replaced at the same rate it is consumed. Natural gas takes millions of years to form from decaying organic matter under high pressure and temperature, but we are extracting and burning it at a much faster rate.

steps.title: Identify the disadvantage
steps.content:
A major disadvantage of relying on natural gas is its **non-renewable nature**. Because there is a finite supply, it will eventually run out, making it an unsustainable energy source for long-term global demand.

Final Answer: It is a non-renewable (finite) resource that will eventually be depleted.

steps.title: Identify natural gas composition
steps.content: Natural gas consists primarily of **methane** ($CH_4$). While burning natural gas produces less carbon dioxide ($CO_2$) than burning coal, the extraction and transport of the gas itself present a different environmental challenge. This topic is closely related to **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Consider extraction and transport
steps.content: During the processes of hydraulic fracturing (fracking), drilling, and transport through long-distance pipelines, methane can escape into the atmosphere. These are known as "fugitive emissions."

steps.title: Analyze the greenhouse effect
steps.content: Methane is a significantly more potent greenhouse gas than carbon dioxide. Over a 20-year period, methane is estimated to be over 80 times more effective at trapping heat in the atmosphere than $CO_2$. Therefore, even small percentages of leakage can cancel out the climate benefits of switching from coal to gas.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The disadvantage of relying on natural gas is the **risk of methane leakage** during extraction and transport, which releases a greenhouse gas far more potent than carbon dioxide into the atmosphere.

According to the markscheme, you would earn the mark by mentioning this specific concern.

parts.content: Write the balanced equation for the incomplete combustion of propane forming carbon monoxide.
parts.markscheme:
Balanced equation for the incomplete combustion:
$2\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 7\text{O}_2 \to 6\text{CO} + 8\text{H}_2\text{O}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify reactants and products
steps.content:
First, we identify the chemical species involved. The question specifies the **incomplete combustion** of **propane**, which produces **carbon monoxide** and water.

*   Reactants: Propane ($C_3H_8$) and Oxygen ($O_2$)
*   Products: Carbon monoxide ($CO$) and Water ($H_2O$)

We start with the unbalanced skeleton equation:
$$C_3H_8 + O_2 \to CO + H_2O$$

steps.title: Balance Carbon atoms first
steps.content:
Following the inspection method, we balance Carbon (C) first. There are 3 carbon atoms in propane ($C_3H_8$), so we need 3 molecules of carbon monoxide ($CO$) on the product side.

$$C_3H_8 + O_2 \to 3CO + H_2O$$

steps.title: Balance Hydrogen atoms
steps.content:
Next, we balance Hydrogen (H). Propane has 8 hydrogen atoms. Since each water molecule ($H_2O$) has 2 hydrogen atoms, we need 4 water molecules ($4 \times 2 = 8$).

$$C_3H_8 + O_2 \to 3CO + 4H_2O$$

steps.title: Balance Oxygen atoms
steps.content:
Now, let's count the oxygen atoms on the product side:
*   $3$ from $3CO$
*   $4$ from $4H_2O$
*   Total = $7$ oxygen atoms.

To get 7 atoms from $O_2$ molecules on the reactant side, we use the fraction $\frac{7}{2}$ (or 3.5):

$$C_3H_8 + 3.5O_2 \to 3CO + 4H_2O$$

steps.title: Convert to whole numbers
steps.content:
IB chemistry equations typically require the smallest whole-number coefficients. To remove the $3.5$ fraction, we multiply the entire equation by 2.

$$\begin{aligned} 2 \times (C_3H_8 + 3.5O_2 &\to 3CO + 4H_2O) \\ 2C_3H_8 + 7O_2 &\to 6CO + 8H_2O \end{aligned}$$

This matches the markscheme requirement for the final balanced equation.

steps.title: Identify reactants and products
steps.content:
To write the balanced equation, we first identify the chemical formulas for the reactants and products. The question specifies the **incomplete combustion of propane** ($C_3H_8$) with oxygen ($O_2$) to form **carbon monoxide** ($CO$) and water ($H_2O$).

This falls under **Topic R2.1: How much? The amount of chemical change** in your syllabus, where we focus on stoichiometry and balancing equations.

highlights.text: incomplete combustion of propane forming carbon monoxide

steps.title: Assign algebraic variables
steps.content:
In the Algebraic Balancing Method, we assign a variable ($a, b, c, d$) to the coefficient of each substance in the skeleton equation:

$$aC_3H_8 + bO_2 \to cCO + dH_2O$$

steps.title: Set up element equations
steps.content:
Based on the Law of Conservation of Mass, the number of atoms of each element must be equal on both sides of the equation. We create an equation for each element:

*   **Carbon (C):** $3a = c$
*   **Hydrogen (H):** $8a = 2d$
*   **Oxygen (O):** $2b = c + d$

steps.title: Solve for the coefficients
steps.content:
Let's assume $a = 1$ to solve the system:
1.  From the Carbon equation: $c = 3(1) = 3$
2.  From the Hydrogen equation: $2d = 8(1) \implies d = 4$
3.  From the Oxygen equation: $2b = 3 + 4 \implies 2b = 7 \implies b = 3.5$

Our coefficients are currently: $a=1, b=3.5, c=3, d=4$.

steps.title: Convert to integer coefficients
steps.content:
In chemical equations, we prefer the simplest whole-number coefficients. Since $b = 3.5$, we multiply all coefficients by **2** to eliminate the decimal:

*   $a = 1 \times 2 = 2$
*   $b = 3.5 \times 2 = 7$
*   $c = 3 \times 2 = 6$
*   $d = 4 \times 2 = 8$

steps.title: Final balanced equation
steps.content:
Substitute the integer coefficients back into the equation. 

$$2C_3H_8 + 7O_2 \to 6CO + 8H_2O$$

This matches the requirement for forming carbon monoxide during incomplete combustion and ensures all atoms are balanced (6 Carbon, 16 Hydrogen, and 14 Oxygen on each side).

questions.specification: The molar enthalpy of combustion of propane is $-2220\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. What is the enthalpy change, in kJ, when $0.500\text{ mol}$ of propane is burned?

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Involves interpreting enthalpy change as energy transfer (negative for exothermic combustion) when a reaction occurs for a specified amount.

options.markscheme:
The enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) is calculated by multiplying the number of moles ($n$) by the molar enthalpy of combustion ($\Delta H_c$):
$\Delta H = n \times \Delta H_c$
$\Delta H = 0.500\text{ mol} \times (-2220\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}) = -1110\text{ kJ}$
Combustion is an exothermic process, so the sign must be negative. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Incorrect sign. Combustion is an exothermic process, therefore the enthalpy change must be negative ($\Delta H < 0$). This value represents the energy released as a magnitude, but not the enthalpy change.
Incorrect magnitude. This would be the result of dividing the molar enthalpy by the number of moles ($\frac{-2220}{0.500}$) instead of multiplying.
Incorrect magnitude and sign. This would be the result of an incorrect calculation (division) and the wrong sign for an exothermic process.

parts.content: Explain how the principle of conservation of energy applies to calorimetry experiments like the one described.
parts.markscheme:
- State that energy released from the combustion of ethanol is absorbed by the water, increasing its temperature. <marks>A1</marks>
- State that the total energy released by the fuel is equal to the energy absorbed by the water, assuming no energy loss to the surroundings. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Understand the energy flow
steps.content: In a calorimetry experiment, we are measuring the energy change of a chemical reaction. When the ethanol burns in the spirit burner, it undergoes an exothermic combustion reaction. This means the chemical potential energy stored in the ethanol's bonds is released as thermal energy (heat). This process is covered in **Topic R1.1: Measuring enthalpy change**.

steps.title: Energy absorption by water
steps.content: The principle of conservation of energy applies because the thermal energy released from the combustion of the ethanol is transferred directly to the water. As the water molecules absorb this energy, their average kinetic energy increases, which results in the observed increase in the water's temperature.

steps.title: Apply conservation principle
steps.content:
The Law of Conservation of Energy states that energy cannot be created or destroyed. In this context, it implies that the total energy released by the fuel is equal to the total energy absorbed by the water, provided we assume there is no energy loss to the surroundings (like the air or the beaker). 

To earn full marks, you should mention:
1. The energy released by the alcohol is absorbed by the water (increasing its temperature).
2. The total energy released by the fuel equals the energy absorbed by the water (assuming no losses).

steps.title: Define system and surroundings
steps.content:
To explain the conservation of energy in this experiment, we first define our thermodynamic boundaries:

*   **The System**: The chemical reaction occurring in the spirit burner (the combustion of the alcohol).
*   **The Surroundings**: Everything else, primarily the water in the calorimeter and the calorimeter itself.

In an exothermic reaction like combustion, energy is transferred from the system to the surroundings.

steps.title: Describe energy transfer
steps.content: According to the principle of conservation of energy, energy cannot be created or destroyed. Therefore, the thermal energy released by the combustion of the ethanol is absorbed by the water. This absorption of energy is what causes the temperature of the water to increase, as shown in the student's graph.

highlights.text: energy released from the combustion of ethanol is absorbed by the water, increasing its temperature.

steps.title: Apply conservation law
steps.content:
In an ideal calorimetry experiment, we assume there is no energy loss to the wider environment (such as the air or the spirit burner itself). Under this assumption, the law of conservation of energy states that the total energy lost by the system must be exactly equal to the total energy gained by the surroundings.

Mathematically, this is expressed as:
$$q_{\text{system}} + q_{\text{surroundings}} = 0$$
$$\text{Energy released by fuel} = \text{Energy absorbed by water}$$

highlights.text: total energy released by the fuel is equal to the energy absorbed by the water, assuming no energy loss to the surroundings.

parts.content: State two possible sources of error in the experiment and explain how they could affect the calculated enthalpy change.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify that heat loss to the surroundings (e.g., to the air, container) would result in a lower calculated enthalpy change (less exothermic). <marks>A1</marks>
- Identify that incomplete combustion of ethanol would also lead to a lower calculated enthalpy change (less exothermic), as less energy is released per mole. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the experiment type
steps.content: This experiment is a calorimetry investigation into the enthalpy of combustion. In these types of practicals, we measure the temperature change of water to calculate the energy released by a fuel. To understand the errors, we need to look at **Topic R1.1: Measuring enthalpy change** and specifically how experimental setups differ from ideal conditions.

steps.title: Error 1: Heat loss
steps.content:
The most significant error in a spirit burner experiment is **heat loss to the surroundings**. 

Not all the energy released by the burning ethanol is transferred to the water. Some heat energy is lost to the surrounding air, the tripod, the gauze, or the copper bit (container) itself. This means the water does not absorb the full amount of energy produced by the reaction.

highlights.text: Identify that heat loss to the surroundings (e.g., to the air, container) would result in a lower calculated enthalpy change (less exothermic).

steps.title: Effect of heat loss
steps.content:
Because heat is lost to the environment, the measured temperature increase ($\Delta T$) will be **lower** than it should be. 

Since the heat energy ($q$) is calculated using:

$$q = m c \Delta T$$

A smaller $\Delta T$ results in a smaller calculated value for $q$. Consequently, the calculated enthalpy of combustion ($\Delta H_c$) will be **less exothermic** (a smaller negative value) than the theoretical literature value.

steps.title: Error 2: Incomplete combustion
steps.content:
Another source of error is the **incomplete combustion** of the alcohol. In an open laboratory setting, there may not be enough oxygen reaching the wick for complete combustion to occur. 

You can often see this as a yellow/orange flame or soot (carbon) forming on the bottom of the beaker. Instead of producing only $\mathrm{CO_2}$ and $\mathrm{H_2O}$, the reaction might produce $\mathrm{CO}$ or $\mathrm{C}$.

highlights.text: Identify that incomplete combustion of ethanol would also lead to a lower calculated enthalpy change (less exothermic), as less energy is released per mole.

steps.title: Effect of incomplete combustion
steps.content:
Incomplete combustion releases **less energy per mole** of fuel compared to complete combustion. 

Because less energy is physically being released by the chemical reaction, the temperature of the water will not rise as much as expected. Similar to the heat loss error, this results in a **lower calculated enthalpy change** (the value appears less exothermic than it truly is).

steps.title: Identify experimental errors
steps.content: In calorimetry experiments involving spirit burners, the experimental value for the enthalpy of combustion is almost always lower (less exothermic) than the theoretical value found in data tables. We need to identify two specific reasons for this discrepancy and explain the underlying chemistry.

highlights.text: State two possible sources of error in the experiment and explain how they could affect the calculated enthalpy change.

steps.title: Error 1: Heat loss
steps.content:
The most significant error is **heat loss to the surroundings**. 

In this setup, we assume all the energy released by the combustion of the alcohol is transferred directly to the water. However, in reality, a large portion of the heat is lost to the surrounding air or used to heat the copper can (calorimeter) itself. Since the temperature increase ($\Delta T$) of the water is lower than it should be, the calculated heat energy ($Q$) using $$Q = mc\Delta T$$ will be smaller. Consequently, the calculated enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) will be **less exothermic** (a smaller negative value) than the true value.

steps.title: Error 2: Incomplete combustion
steps.content:
A second major error is **incomplete combustion**. This occurs because the spirit burner often has a restricted supply of oxygen. 

Instead of the alcohol reacting completely to form carbon dioxide ($CO_2$), it may form carbon monoxide ($CO$) or soot (carbon, $C$). This is a problem because the formation of $CO$ or soot releases significantly less energy per mole of fuel than the formation of $CO_2$. This concept is covered in **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**. 

Because less energy is released during the reaction, the water temperature doesn't rise as much as expected for complete combustion. This results in a **less exothermic** experimental enthalpy value.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn the full 2 marks, you must both identify the error and explain its effect on the result:

1.  **Heat loss:** Leads to a lower temperature rise, resulting in a **less exothermic** (lower) calculated enthalpy change.
2.  **Incomplete combustion:** Less energy is released per mole of fuel compared to complete combustion, also resulting in a **less exothermic** calculated enthalpy change.

steps.title: Analyze the enthalpy calculation
steps.content:
To understand how errors affect the result, we first look at the calculation for the enthalpy of combustion ($\Delta H_c$):

1. Calculate the heat absorbed by the water: $q = mc\Delta T$
2. Calculate the moles of fuel burned: $n = \frac{m_{\text{fuel}}}{M}$
3. Calculate enthalpy change: $$\Delta H_c = -\frac{q}{n}$$

Any error that changes the measured values of $q$ or $n$ will shift the final $\Delta H_c$ value.

steps.title: Error 1: Alcohol evaporation
steps.content:
A significant source of error in spirit burner experiments is the **evaporation of the alcohol**. 

Alcohol can evaporate from the wick in two main instances:
*   After the initial weighing but before the burner is ignited.
*   After the flame is extinguished but before the final weighing.

This is a systematic error because it consistently occurs due to the volatile nature of alcohols like ethanol.

steps.title: Effect of evaporation
steps.content:
Because some alcohol evaporates without being burned, the measured mass loss (the difference between the initial and final mass) will be **greater** than the mass of fuel that actually released heat to the water.

In the calculation $$\Delta H_c = -\frac{q}{n}$$, the value for $n$ (moles) will be incorrectly **high**. Dividing the heat by a larger number of moles results in a **lower (less exothermic)** calculated enthalpy change.

steps.title: Error 2: Heat loss
steps.content: A second major error is **heat loss to the surroundings**. Not all the energy released by the combustion reaction is transferred to the water; some energy heats the surrounding air and the container (calorimeter) itself.

steps.title: Effect of heat loss
steps.content:
Since heat is lost to the surroundings, the temperature increase ($\Delta T$) of the water is smaller than it should be. This means the calculated value for $q$ is too **low**.

Looking again at $$\Delta H_c = -\frac{q}{n}$$, if $q$ is too small, the resulting enthalpy change will be **lower (less exothermic)** than the theoretical value.

parts.content: Using the graph, determine the amount of heat energy $(q)$ transferred to the water when 200 g of water is heated. Assume the specific heat capacity of water $(c)$ is $4.18 \mathrm{\, J \, g^{-1} \, K^{-1}}$.
parts.markscheme:
From the graph:
- At 200 g of water, temperature increase $= 20^\circ \mathrm{C}$.

- Substitute values into the formula $q=mc\Delta T$: 
$$q = (200 \, \mathrm{g}) \times (4.18 \, \mathrm{J \, g^{-1} \, K^{-1}}) \times (20 \, \mathrm{K})$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate final answer: 
$$q = 16,720 \, \mathrm{J} \text{ or } 16.72 \, \mathrm{kJ}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Read the graph
steps.content:
To determine the temperature increase ($\Delta T$), we look at the graph provided. Locate the value of **200 g** on the x-axis (mass of water). Move vertically up to the line of best fit and then horizontally to the y-axis to find the corresponding temperature increase.

From the graph, for a mass of $200\text{ g}$, the temperature increase is:
$$\Delta T = 20^\circ\text{C}$$

steps.title: Identify the formula
steps.content:
To calculate the heat energy ($q$) transferred, we use the calorimetry formula from **Topic R1.1 (Measuring enthalpy change)**:

$$q = mc\Delta T$$

Where:
- $m$ is the mass of the water ($200\text{ g}$)
- $c$ is the specific heat capacity of water ($4.18\text{ J g}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$)
- $\Delta T$ is the change in temperature ($20\text{ K}$)

Note: A change of $1^\circ\text{C}$ is equivalent to a change of $1\text{ K}$, so we can use $20\text{ K}$ directly.

highlights.text: specific heat capacity of water (c) is 4.18 J g^-1 K^-1

steps.title: Calculate the heat energy
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values into the formula to find $q$:

$$\begin{aligned} q &= 200\text{ g} \times 4.18\text{ J g}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1} \times 20\text{ K} \\ q &= 16,720\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

To express this in kilojoules (kJ), divide by $1,000$:

$$q = 16.72\text{ kJ}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the three values on the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication directly on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Enter the expression into a new cell to get the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the total heat energy by multiplying mass, specific heat capacity, and temperature change.

steps.title: Analyze the graph
steps.content:
To find the heat energy, we first need to determine the temperature increase ($\Delta T$) that corresponds to the given mass of water. 

Looking at the graph, locate the value of $200\text{ g}$ on the horizontal axis ($x$-axis). Move vertically to the line of best fit and then horizontally to the vertical axis ($y$-axis) to find the temperature change.

steps.title: Extract data points
steps.content:
From the graph, we can see that when the mass of water is $200\text{ g}$, the temperature increase is:

$$\Delta T = 20^{\circ}\text{C}$$

steps.title: Calculate the mass-temperature product
steps.content:
Using the **Proportional Relationship Method**, we recognize that for a fixed amount of heat energy, mass ($m$) and temperature change ($\Delta T$) are inversely proportional. The product of these two variables represents the total thermal 'load' before considering the specific heat capacity.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Product} &= m \times \Delta T \\ &= 200\text{ g} \times 20\text{ K} \\ &= 4000\text{ g K} \end{aligned}$$

*(Note: A change of $1^{\circ}\text{C}$ is equivalent to a change of $1\text{ K}$.)*

ti84.instructions: Enter 200 * 20 on the main screen and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the product of mass and temperature change.

steps.title: Determine heat energy (q)
steps.content:
Now, we multiply this product by the specific heat capacity ($c$) to find the total heat energy ($q$). This follows the fundamental calorimetric equation $q = mc\Delta T$.

$$\begin{aligned} q &= c \times (m \Delta T) \\ &= 4.18\text{ J g}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1} \times 4000\text{ g K} \\ &= 16,720\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 4.18 * 4000 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Multiply the specific heat capacity by the previously calculated product.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The heat energy transferred is $16,720\text{ J}$. In chemistry, we often express this in kilojoules (kJ) by dividing by $1000$.

$$q = 16.72\text{ kJ}$$

According to the markscheme, identifying the correct $\Delta T$ from the graph and substituting into the formula earns the first mark (**M1**), and the correct calculation leads to the second mark (**A1**).

parts.content: If the student burned 1.00 g of ethanol to produce the energy calculated in Part 3, determine the enthalpy change of combustion of ethanol in $\mathrm{kJ \, mol^{-1}}$. The molar mass of ethanol is $46.08 \mathrm{\, g \, mol^{-1}}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate moles of ethanol:
$$\text{Moles of ethanol} = \frac{\text{mass}}{\text{molar mass}} = \frac{1.00}{46.08} = 0.0217 \, \mathrm{mol}$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute values into the enthalpy change formula:
$$\Delta H_c = \frac{\text{energy released}}{\text{moles}} = \frac{16.72}{0.0217}$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate final answer (negative sign required for exothermic reaction):
$$\Delta H_c = -770.5 \, \mathrm{kJ \, mol^{-1}}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify required information
steps.content:
To find the enthalpy change of combustion ($\Delta H_c$) in $\mathrm{kJ \, mol^{-1}}$, we need two pieces of information:
1. The amount of energy released (from Part 3, this was $16.72 \, \mathrm{kJ}$).
2. The amount of ethanol burned in moles ($n$).

We are given the mass of ethanol burned and its molar mass.

steps.title: Calculate moles of ethanol
steps.content:
First, we convert the mass of ethanol to moles using the formula from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

$$\text{moles} = \frac{1.00 \, \mathrm{g}}{46.08 \, \mathrm{g \, mol^{-1}}} \approx 0.0217 \, \mathrm{mol}$$

This calculation follows the principles of counting particles covered in **Structure 1.4: The mole**.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1.00 / 46.08 on the home screen and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate moles of ethanol

steps.title: Calculate enthalpy change
steps.content:
Now, we divide the energy released by the number of moles to find the energy per mole:

$$\Delta H_c = \frac{\text{energy released}}{\text{moles}}$$

$$\Delta H_c = \frac{16.72 \, \mathrm{kJ}}{0.0217 \, \mathrm{mol}} \approx 770.5 \, \mathrm{kJ \, mol^{-1}}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 16.72 / 0.0217 on the home screen and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Divide energy by moles

steps.title: Determine final sign
steps.content:
Since combustion is an **exothermic** reaction (energy is released to the surroundings), the enthalpy change $\Delta H$ must be negative. 

$$\Delta H_c = -770.5 \, \mathrm{kJ \, mol^{-1}}$$

You would earn marks for correctly calculating the moles (M1), correctly substituting into the enthalpy formula (M1), and providing the final value with the correct negative sign and units (A1).

steps.title: Identify given data
steps.content:
To find the enthalpy change of combustion ($\\Delta H_c$), we first identify the energy released and the mass of the substance that produced it. From Part 3, we know the energy released ($q$) is $16.72 \\mathrm{\, kJ}$. 

The problem provides the mass burned and the molar mass:
- Mass of ethanol ($m$) = $1.00 \\mathrm{\, g}$
- Molar mass of ethanol ($M$) = $46.08 \\mathrm{\, g \, mol^{-1}}$

steps.title: Set up mass proportionality
steps.content:
Since $\\Delta H_c$ is defined as the heat energy change per **one mole**, we can use the **Mass Proportionality Approach**. 

One mole of ethanol weighs exactly $46.08 \\mathrm{\, g}$. We can set up a ratio to scale the energy released from $1.00 \\mathrm{\, g}$ up to the mass of one full mole ($46.08 \\mathrm{\, g}$):

$$\\frac{\\text{Energy released}}{\\text{Mass burned}} = \\frac{\\Delta H_c}{\\text{Molar mass}}$$

steps.title: Calculate molar energy
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the known values into our proportion to solve for the energy released by one mole ($x$):

$$\\frac{16.72 \\mathrm{\, kJ}}{1.00 \\mathrm{\, g}} = \\frac{x}{46.08 \\mathrm{\, g \, mol^{-1}}}$$

$$x = 16.72 \\times 46.08 = 770.4576 \\mathrm{\, kJ \, mol^{-1}}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the product of the energy and the molar mass.

calculator.description: Calculate the total energy for one mole by multiplying the energy per gram by the molar mass.

steps.title: Apply sign and round
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, the enthalpy change of combustion ($\\Delta H_c$) is always **negative** because combustion is an exothermic process (energy is released to the surroundings).

Rounding to four significant figures (as suggested by the markscheme and the precision of the inputs):

$$\\Delta H_c = -770.5 \\mathrm{\, kJ \, mol^{-1}}$$
