parts.content: Outline why the compounds in the table are known as hydrocarbons.
parts.markscheme: They are composed of carbon and hydrogen only. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Interpret the term
steps.content: To understand why these are called **hydrocarbons**, we can start by looking at the name itself. It is a compound word formed from **hydrogen** and **carbon**. This gives us a direct clue about the chemical composition of the molecules listed in the table.

steps.title: Analyze chemical composition
steps.content:
Using the **Chemical Composition Analysis** approach, we examine the molecular formulas provided in the data table:

- Ethane: $C_2H_6$
- Ethene: $C_2H_4$
- Butane: $C_4H_{10}$
- Hexene: $C_6H_{12}$

Notice that every single formula in the list contains only the symbols $C$ (carbon) and $H$ (hydrogen). There are no other elements, such as oxygen or nitrogen, present in any of these structures.

steps.title: State the definition
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, specifically in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**, we define a hydrocarbon based on this observation. A hydrocarbon is a compound that is composed of **carbon and hydrogen atoms only**.

According to the markscheme, the key requirement for this mark is to mention that these compounds contain *only* these two specific elements.

parts.content: State the structural feature of the alkenes ethene, butene and hexene.
parts.markscheme: Carbon-carbon double bond / $\text{C}=\text{C}$ <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the naming pattern
steps.content:
To solve this, we first look at the names of the specific compounds mentioned in the question: **ethene**, **butene**, and **hexene**. 

In organic chemistry, the suffix (the ending of the name) tells us which **homologous series** the compound belongs to. All three of these compounds end in **-ene**.

steps.title: Recognize the homologous series
steps.content:
As covered in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**, the suffix **-ene** is the identifying marker for the **alkene** homologous series. 

Members of the same homologous series share the same general formula and, most importantly, the same **functional group** or structural feature.

steps.title: Identify the structural feature
steps.content:
The defining structural feature of all alkenes is the presence of at least one **carbon-carbon double bond**. 

In your IB exams, you can write this out in words or show it as a chemical symbol:

$$\text{C}=\text{C}$$

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
Based on the markscheme, the correct answer is simply stating the feature that makes these molecules alkenes.

**Answer:**
Carbon-carbon double bond (or $C=C$)

steps.title: Compare molecular formulas
steps.content:
To find the shared structural feature, let's compare the molecular formulas of the alkanes and alkenes provided in the table for a given number of carbon atoms:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Ethane } (C_2H_6) &\rightarrow \text{Ethene } (C_2H_4) \\
\text{Butane } (C_4H_{10}) &\rightarrow \text{Butene } (C_4H_8) \\
\text{Hexane } (C_6H_{14}) &\rightarrow \text{Hexene } (C_6H_{12})
\end{aligned}$$

Notice that in every case, the alkene has exactly two fewer hydrogen atoms than the corresponding alkane.

steps.title: Analyze the H:C ratio
steps.content:
Alkanes are **saturated** hydrocarbons with the general formula $C_nH_{2n+2}$. 

The alkenes in the table follow the general formula $C_nH_{2n}$. This lower hydrogen-to-carbon ratio indicates a **degree of unsaturation**. For every two hydrogens "missing" compared to the saturated alkane, the molecule must contain a structural feature that allows carbon to maintain its four required bonds without those extra hydrogens.

steps.title: Identify the structural feature
steps.content:
To satisfy the valency of carbon (which must form 4 bonds), the "missing" hydrogen atoms are compensated for by the presence of a **carbon-carbon double bond** ($C=C$). 

This is the defining functional group for the alkene homologous series, as covered in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: The structural feature common to ethene, butene, and hexene is the **carbon-carbon double bond** (or $C=C$).

parts.content: Explain why hexane is a liquid at $20^\circ\text{C}$ and ethane is a gas.
parts.markscheme:
Hexane has a higher molar mass <marks>1</marks>

and stronger London dispersion forces than ethane <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify intermolecular forces
steps.content:
To understand why hexane and ethane have different states of matter, we first need to look at their structure. Both hexane ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{14}$) and ethane ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$) are non-polar hydrocarbons. 

Because they are non-polar, the only intermolecular forces acting between their molecules are **London dispersion forces (LDF)**. This is a key concept from **Topic S2.2.8: Intermolecular forces**.

steps.title: Compare molar masses
steps.content:
The strength of London dispersion forces depends heavily on the size of the molecule. We can quantify this by comparing their molar masses using the periodic table values ($C = 12.01, H = 1.01$):

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Molar mass of ethane } (\text{C}_2\text{H}_6) &= 2(12.01) + 6(1.01) = 30.08 \text{ g mol}^{-1} \\ \text{Molar mass of hexane } (\text{C}_6\text{H}_{14}) &= 6(12.01) + 14(1.01) = 86.20 \text{ g mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Hexane has a significantly **higher molar mass** than ethane.

gdc.instructions: Enter the atomic mass sums: 2*12.01 + 6*1.01 for ethane and 6*12.01 + 14*1.01 for hexane.

ti84.instructions: Enter the atomic mass sums: 2*12.01 + 6*1.01 for ethane and 6*12.01 + 14*1.01 for hexane.

desmos.instructions: Enter the atomic mass sums: 2*12.01 + 6*1.01 for ethane and 6*12.01 + 14*1.01 for hexane.

calculator.description: Verify the molar mass calculations.

steps.title: Relate size to LDF strength
steps.content:
Since hexane is a larger molecule with more electrons, it has a larger electron cloud. This makes the molecule more polarizable, which leads to **stronger London dispersion forces** compared to those in ethane.

In the IB markscheme, you earn the first mark for identifying the higher molar mass and the second mark for connecting it to stronger LDF.

steps.title: Connect to state of matter
steps.content:
Stronger intermolecular forces require more energy to overcome. Because hexane's LDF are much stronger than ethane's, its boiling point ($69^\circ\text{C}$) is much higher than room temperature ($20^\circ\text{C}$), meaning it stays as a **liquid**. 

In contrast, ethane's weak LDF result in a boiling point of $-89^\circ\text{C}$, so it has already boiled and exists as a **gas** at room temperature.

steps.title: Compare chain length
steps.content:
To understand why these substances have different states of matter, we first look at their molecular structures. Hexane ($C_6H_{14}$) has a much longer carbon chain than ethane ($C_2H_6$). 

Because hexane has more atoms, it also has a significantly higher number of electrons and a larger molar mass. This is a key concept covered in **Topic S3.2.4: Physical trends in homologous series**.

steps.title: Surface area and polarizability
steps.content:
The longer chain in hexane provides a larger **surface area** for contact between molecules. Combined with the higher number of electrons, this makes the electron cloud in hexane more **polarizable** (easier to distort). 

Greater polarizability leads to more frequent and stronger temporary dipoles forming between molecules.

steps.title: Identify intermolecular forces
steps.content:
Both molecules are non-polar hydrocarbons, so they only experience **London dispersion forces** (LDFs). 

Because hexane has a larger surface area and more electrons, its London dispersion forces are significantly stronger than those in ethane. This aligns with **Topic S2.2.8: Intermolecular forces** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Link to boiling point
steps.content:
Stronger intermolecular forces require more energy to overcome. 

As seen in the table, hexane's boiling point ($+69^\circ\text{C}$) is much higher than room temperature ($20^\circ\text{C}$), so it remains a **liquid**. Ethane's boiling point ($-89^\circ\text{C}$) is far below room temperature, so it has already turned into a **gas**.

parts.content: Deduce the molecular and empirical formula of propene.
parts.markscheme:
Empirical formula: $\text{CH}_2$ <marks>1</marks>

Molecular formula: $\text{C}_3\text{H}_6$ <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the homologous series
steps.content: First, we look at the name **propene**. The suffix **-ene** tells us that this compound belongs to the **alkene** homologous series. Alkenes are hydrocarbons that contain at least one carbon-carbon double bond.

steps.title: Determine the carbon count
steps.content: Next, we use IUPAC nomenclature rules (which you can review in **Topic S3.2.5**) to identify the number of carbon atoms from the prefix. The prefix **prop-** indicates that there are exactly **3 carbon atoms** in the molecule ($n = 3$).

steps.title: Find the molecular formula
steps.content:
The general formula for any alkene is $$C_nH_{2n}$$. Since we know $n = 3$ for propene, we can substitute this value into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Number of C atoms} &= 3 \\ \text{Number of H atoms} &= 2 \times 3 = 6 \end{aligned}$$

This gives us the **molecular formula**: $$\text{C}_3\text{H}_6$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply 2 by 3

calculator.description: Calculate the number of hydrogen atoms for n=3

steps.title: Simplify for empirical formula
steps.content:
The **empirical formula** is the simplest whole-number ratio of the atoms in a compound (as covered in **Topic S1.4.4**). 

For $\text{C}_3\text{H}_6$, the ratio of $C:H$ is $3:6$. We can simplify this by dividing both numbers by their greatest common divisor, which is 3:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{C} &= \frac{3}{3} = 1 \\ \text{H} &= \frac{6}{3} = 2 \end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the **empirical formula** is: $$\text{CH}_2$$

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content: The molecular formula is $$\text{C}_3\text{H}_6$$ and the empirical formula is $$\text{CH}_2$$. You earn 1 mark for each correct formula.

steps.title: Interpret the name "propene"
steps.content:
To deduce the formula, we first look at the name of the hydrocarbon. In IUPAC nomenclature (which you can review in **Topic S3.2.5**):
- The prefix **"prop-"** indicates that the molecule contains exactly **three carbon atoms**.
- The suffix **"-ene"** indicates that it belongs to the alkene homologous series, meaning it contains at least one **carbon-carbon double bond** ($C=C$).

steps.title: Sketch the structural diagram
steps.content:
We can now sketch the structure. Carbon atoms must always form four bonds to be stable. We place three carbons in a chain with one double bond:

1. Start with the carbon chain: $C=C-C$
2. Add hydrogen atoms so each Carbon has 4 bonds total:
   - The first carbon is double-bonded, so it needs **2 hydrogens**.
   - The middle carbon has a double and a single bond (3 bonds), so it needs **1 hydrogen**.
   - The last carbon is single-bonded, so it needs **3 hydrogens**.

The structure looks like this: 

$$CH_2=CH-CH_3$$

steps.title: Determine the molecular formula
steps.content:
The **molecular formula** shows the actual number of atoms of each element in the molecule. By counting the atoms in our structural sketch:
- Total Carbon (C) atoms = $1 + 1 + 1 = 3$
- Total Hydrogen (H) atoms = $2 + 1 + 3 = 6$

Therefore, the molecular formula is:

$$C_3H_6$$

steps.title: Determine the empirical formula
steps.content:
The **empirical formula** is the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms of each element in a compound. We take the subscripts from the molecular formula ($C_3H_6$) and divide them by their greatest common divisor, which is 3.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Ratio of C} &= \frac{3}{3} = 1 \\ \text{Ratio of H} &= \frac{6}{3} = 2 \end{aligned}$$

This gives us a $1:2$ ratio, so the empirical formula is:

$$CH_2$$

gdc.instructions: Divide 6 by 3 to simplify the ratio.

ti84.instructions: Divide the number of hydrogens by the number of carbons to find the ratio.

calculator.description: Simplify the atom ratio by dividing by the greatest common divisor (3).

steps.title: Identify the pattern
steps.content:
First, let's look at the compounds in the table that end in **-ene**. By looking at their molecular formulas, we can see a clear relationship between the number of carbon ($C$) and hydrogen ($H$) atoms:

*   **Ethene**: $C_2H_4$ ($2 \times 2 = 4$)
*   **Butene**: $C_4H_8$ ($4 \times 2 = 8$)
*   **Hexene**: $C_6H_{12}$ ($6 \times 2 = 12$)

In every case, the number of hydrogen atoms is exactly **double** the number of carbon atoms. This gives us a ratio of $1:2$.

steps.title: Determine carbon count
steps.content:
Next, we need to determine how many carbon atoms are in **propene**. 

Following the naming convention (IUPAC nomenclature) shown in the table:
*   **Eth-** means 2 carbons
*   **But-** means 4 carbons
*   **Hex-** means 6 carbons

The prefix **Prop-** represents the 3-carbon intermediate in this sequence. Therefore, propene has **3 carbon atoms**.

steps.title: Deduce molecular formula
steps.content:
Now we apply the $1:2$ ratio we identified in Step 0 to the 3 carbon atoms:

$$\text{Number of H atoms} = 3 \times 2 = 6$$

So, the **molecular formula** for propene is **$C_3H_6$**.

ti84.instructions: Enter the number of carbons and multiply by 2.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of hydrogen atoms using the 1:2 ratio for 3 carbons.

steps.title: Find empirical formula
steps.content:
The **empirical formula** is the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms in a compound. 

For $C_3H_6$, we can divide both subscripts by their greatest common divisor, which is 3:

$$\frac{C_3}{3} \rightarrow C_1$$
$$\frac{H_6}{3} \rightarrow H_2$$

This gives us an **empirical formula** of **$CH_2$**.

parts.content: Predict the physical state of propene at $20^\circ\text{C}$, giving a reason for your answer.
parts.markscheme: Gas because butene, with four carbon atoms in the longest chain, is also a gas. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify propene in the series
steps.content:
First, let's identify what propene is. Propene ($C_3H_6$) is an alkene with 3 carbon atoms. In the provided table, we can see data for other alkenes: ethene ($C_2H_4$) and butene ($C_4H_8$).

This question is related to **Topic S3.2.4: Physical trends in homologous series**, where we study how properties like boiling point change as the carbon chain gets longer.

steps.title: Analyze the surrounding data
steps.content:
To predict the state of propene, we can look at the alkenes that are slightly smaller and slightly larger than it:

*   **Ethene ($C_2H_4$):** 2 carbons, Boiling Point = $-104^\circ\text{C}$ (Gas at $20^\circ\text{C}$)
*   **Butene ($C_4H_8$):** 4 carbons, Boiling Point = $-6^\circ\text{C}$ (Gas at $20^\circ\text{C}$)

Propene ($C_3H_6$) sits directly between these two in the homologous series.

steps.title: Interpolate the physical state
steps.content:
Interpolation involves estimating a value that falls between two known values. 

Since ethene (2 carbons) is a gas and butene (4 carbons) is also a gas at $20^\circ\text{C}$, we can logically conclude that the 3-carbon alkene between them must also be a gas at that same temperature.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The physical state of propene at $20^\circ\text{C}$ is **gas**.

**Reason:** Propene contains 3 carbons, which is between ethene (2 carbons) and butene (4 carbons). Since both of these reference points are gases at room temperature, propene must also be a gas.

steps.title: Identify the trend
steps.content: To predict the state of propene ($C_3H_6$), we look at the relationship between the number of carbon atoms and the boiling point in a homologous series. As discussed in **Topic S2.2.9: Intermolecular forces and physical properties**, the boiling point of hydrocarbons increases as the carbon chain length increases.

steps.title: Explain London dispersion forces
steps.content:
Hydrocarbons are non-polar, so the only intermolecular forces acting between them are **London dispersion forces**. 

As the number of carbon atoms increases, the molar mass and the size of the electron cloud increase. This leads to stronger London dispersion forces, which require more energy to overcome, resulting in a higher boiling point.

steps.title: Compare propene to butene
steps.content:
Let's compare propene ($C_3H_6$) to the next alkene in the series provided in the table: butene ($C_4H_8$).

* Butene has 4 carbons and a boiling point of $-6^\circ\text{C}$.
* Propene has only 3 carbons.

Because propene has a shorter carbon chain, it has weaker London dispersion forces than butene. Therefore, its boiling point must be lower than $-6^\circ\text{C}$.

steps.title: Determine the physical state
steps.content:
We are asked for the state at room temperature ($20^\circ\text{C}$). 

If butene is already a gas at $20^\circ\text{C}$ (because its boiling point of $-6^\circ\text{C}$ is much lower than room temperature), and propene has an even lower boiling point, then propene must also be a gas.

steps.title: Final prediction
steps.content:
The physical state of propene at $20^\circ\text{C}$ is **gas**.

**Reasoning:** Propene has a shorter carbon chain than butene, resulting in weaker London dispersion forces and a lower boiling point. Since butene is a gas at room temperature, propene must also be a gas.

parts.content: Deduce equations for the complete and incomplete combustion of ethene.
parts.markscheme:
Complete combustion: $\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ <marks>1</marks>

Incomplete combustion: $\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify reactants and products
steps.content:
To solve this, we use the **Systematic Stoichiometric Balancing** method. First, we identify the chemical formula for ethene ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_4$) from the table. 

In IB Chemistry, combustion reactions follow specific conventions:
*   **Complete combustion**: Occurs in excess oxygen, producing carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$) and water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$).
*   **Incomplete combustion**: Occurs in limited oxygen, producing carbon monoxide ($\text{CO}$) and water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$) (or sometimes carbon/soot, but we follow the markscheme's preference for $\text{CO}$ here).

These concepts are covered in **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**.

steps.title: Balance complete combustion
steps.content:
We start with the skeleton equation for complete combustion:
$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Following the systematic approach, we balance atoms in this order: Carbon (C), then Hydrogen (H), then Oxygen (O).

1.  **Carbon**: 2 carbons on the left, so we need 2 carbons on the right.
    $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
2.  **Hydrogen**: 4 hydrogens on the left, so we need 4 on the right ($2 \times 2 = 4$).
    $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
3.  **Oxygen**: Count the oxygens on the right side:
    $$2(2) + 2(1) = 6$$
    To get 6 oxygens on the left, we need $3\text{O}_2$.

gdc.instructions: Sum the oxygen atoms from the products.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the total oxygen atoms in 2CO2 and 2H2O.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the expression for total oxygen atoms.

calculator.description: Verify oxygen atom count for complete combustion products.

steps.title: Final complete combustion equation
steps.content:
The balanced equation for the complete combustion of ethene is:

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4(\text{g}) + 3\text{O}_2(\text{g}) \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2(\text{g}) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{l})$$

This earns the first mark.

steps.title: Balance incomplete combustion
steps.content:
Now we follow the same steps for incomplete combustion, where $\text{CO}$ is produced instead of $\text{CO}_2$:

1.  **Carbon**: 2 carbons on the left, so we need 2 on the right.
    $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
2.  **Hydrogen**: 4 hydrogens on the left, so we need 4 on the right.
    $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
3.  **Oxygen**: Count the oxygens on the right side:
    $$2(1) + 2(1) = 4$$
    To get 4 oxygens on the left, we need $2\text{O}_2$.

gdc.instructions: Sum the oxygen atoms from the products.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the total oxygen atoms in 2CO and 2H2O.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the expression for total oxygen atoms.

calculator.description: Verify oxygen atom count for incomplete combustion products.

steps.title: Final incomplete combustion equation
steps.content:
The balanced equation for the incomplete combustion of ethene is:

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4(\text{g}) + 2\text{O}_2(\text{g}) \rightarrow 2\text{CO}(\text{g}) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{l})$$

This earns the second mark. Note that state symbols are usually not required in these specific IB questions unless stated, but they are good practice!

parts.content: Outline the difference between the two types of combustion in terms of the amount of energy released.
parts.markscheme: Complete combustion releases more energy per mole than incomplete combustion. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the task
steps.content: The question asks for the difference in energy release between complete and incomplete combustion. This is a fundamental concept in **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**, specifically looking at how the availability of oxygen affects the energy output of a reaction.

steps.title: Analyze oxidation states
steps.content:
In chemistry, the amount of energy released from a fuel is related to the extent of its oxidation. 

*   **Complete combustion** occurs when there is an excess of oxygen, leading to the **full oxidation** of the hydrocarbon into $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
*   **Incomplete combustion** occurs with limited oxygen, resulting in **partial oxidation** (producing carbon monoxide, $\text{CO}$, or carbon soot, $\text{C}$).

steps.title: Compare energy per mole
steps.content: Because the hydrocarbon undergoes full oxidation during complete combustion, the maximum possible chemical potential energy is released. Consequently, complete combustion releases a greater amount of energy per mole than incomplete combustion.

highlights.text: Complete combustion releases more energy per mole than incomplete combustion.

parts.content:
Natural gas (primarily methane) and gasoline (primarily octane) are two common fuels used for energy production. The table below provides their standard enthalpies of combustion:

| Fuel | Formula | Enthalpy of Combustion ($\Delta H_c$) / $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Methane | $\mathrm{CH}_4$ | $-890$ |
| Octane | $\mathrm{C}_8\mathrm{H}_{18}$ | $-5470$ |

Based on the data provided, calculate the energy released per mole of carbon dioxide ($\mathrm{CO}_2$) produced for both fuels and determine which fuel is more 'carbon-efficient' (releases more energy for the same amount of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ emitted).
parts.markscheme:
- Calculation of energy per mole of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ for both fuels: <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Methane: $\frac{890 \text{ kJ}}{1 \text{ mol } \mathrm{CO}_2} = 890 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ of $\mathrm{CO}_2$
  - Octane: $\frac{5470 \text{ kJ}}{8 \text{ mol } \mathrm{CO}_2} = 683.75 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ of $\mathrm{CO}_2$

- Conclusion: <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Methane is more carbon-efficient because it releases significantly more energy ($890 \text{ kJ}$) for every mole of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ released compared to octane ($683.75 \text{ kJ}$).

parts.content: Aside from carbon dioxide, the combustion of fuels in internal combustion engines leads to the formation of nitrogen oxides ($\mathrm{NO}_x$). Explain why these oxides form even though nitrogen is not a component of the fuel, and identify one environmental problem associated with their release.
parts.markscheme:

- Environmental problem (any one): <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Formation of acid rain (due to the production of nitric acid in the atmosphere).
  - Formation of photochemical smog / ground-level ozone.
  - Respiratory irritation or exacerbation of asthma in humans.

parts.content: Outline the difference between complete and incomplete combustion in terms of the amount of energy released.
parts.markscheme: Complete combustion produces more energy per unit mass than incomplete combustion. <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Define the combustion types
steps.content:
Combustion is a process where a fuel reacts with oxygen. The reaction is classified based on oxygen availability:

*   **Complete combustion** occurs when there is an excess supply of oxygen, producing $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
*   **Incomplete combustion** occurs when the oxygen supply is limited, producing $\text{CO}$ (carbon monoxide) and/or $\text{C}$ (soot/carbon) along with water.

steps.title: Compare energy release
steps.content:
In complete combustion, the carbon in the fuel is fully oxidized to its highest oxidation state ($+4$ in $\text{CO}_2$). Because more bonds are formed and the fuel is fully broken down, significantly more energy is released to the surroundings.

In contrast, incomplete combustion results in only partial oxidation of the carbon. Since the chemical potential energy of the fuel is not fully utilized, less energy is released.

steps.title: Final Comparative Statement
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you must provide a direct comparison. 

**Complete combustion releases a greater amount of energy** per mole (or per unit mass) of fuel compared to incomplete combustion.

highlights.text: Complete combustion produces more energy per unit mass than incomplete combustion.

parts.content: State the general formula for the alkanes and use it to determine the molecular formula of a straight-chain alkane with 20 carbon atoms in its longest chain.
parts.markscheme:
$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

$\text{C}_{20}\text{H}_{42}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

parts.content:
The graph below shows the amount of energy released, in $\text{kJ}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$, when the first four members of the alkane and alcohol homologous series are burned. 

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/8fb57d07-d2ea-489c-b466-0d26213a39b4)[Source: https://docbrown.info/page06/dfalkanes14.htm]

Outline two features of successive members of the same homologous series.
parts.markscheme: Same general formula / differ by a $\text{CH}_2$ group / similar chemical properties / gradation in physical properties. <marks> 2 marks </marks>

steps.title: Identify the concept
steps.content:
This question asks for the defining characteristics of a **homologous series**. In organic chemistry, compounds are grouped into families based on shared structural features. This is covered in **Topic S3.2.3: Homologous series** of your syllabus.

The graph provided shows a clear trend: as the number of carbon atoms increases, the energy released during combustion also increases. This predictability is a hallmark of a homologous series.

highlights.text: Outline two features of successive members of the same homologous series.

steps.title: Recall the structural difference
steps.content:
One of the most fundamental rules is that each member in the series differs from the one before or after it by a specific repeating unit. 

Every successive member differs by a **$\text{CH}_2$ group**. 

For example, if you look at the alkanes:
- Methane: $\text{CH}_4$
- Ethane: $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$

The difference between them is exactly one carbon and two hydrogen atoms ($1 \times \text{C}$ and $2 \times \text{H}$).

steps.title: Recall the general formula
steps.content:
Because all members of the series follow the same structural pattern, they can all be described by a single **common general formula**. 

For the two series shown in the graph:
- **Alkanes:** $$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2}$$
- **Alcohols:** $$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+1}\text{OH}$$

Using these formulas, you can determine the molecular formula for any member of the series simply by knowing the number of carbon atoms ($n$).

steps.title: Identify property trends
steps.content:
While the question specifically asks for two features, it is helpful to recognize the others mentioned in the markscheme:
1. **Similar chemical properties:** Because they have the same functional group, they react in similar ways.
2. **Gradation in physical properties:** As the chain length increases, physical properties (like boiling point or the energy released shown in the graph) change in a regular, predictable way.

To earn the **2 marks**, you simply need to state two of these four valid features.

steps.title: Analyze the energy trend
steps.content:
Looking at the provided graph, we can see that as the number of carbon atoms in the chain increases for both alkanes and alcohols, the amount of energy released during combustion increases in a steady, predictable way. This is a classic example of a **gradation in physical properties**.

In a homologous series, physical properties (like boiling point or enthalpy of combustion) change gradually as the molar mass increases.

steps.title: Identify chemical similarities
steps.content:
Even though their sizes differ, all members of the alkane series react similarly because they contain only $C-C$ and $C-H$ single bonds. Likewise, all alcohols contain the $-\text{OH}$ functional group. This results in **similar chemical properties** across the series.

For example, all the alkanes shown in the graph undergo complete combustion to produce carbon dioxide and water.

steps.title: Examine structural features
steps.content:
Structurally, each successive member of a homologous series differs from the previous one by a constant unit: a **$-\text{CH}_2-$ group**.

Because of this repeating unit, the entire series can be described by the **same general formula**. For alkanes, this is:

$$C_nH_{2n+2}$$

And for the primary alcohols shown:

$$C_nH_{2n+1}OH$$

steps.title: Select two features
steps.content:
To earn the 2 marks for this question, you can outline any two of the following features:
1. They have the **same general formula**.
2. Successive members **differ by a $-\text{CH}_2-$ group**.
3. They show **similar chemical properties** (due to the same functional group).
4. They show a **gradation in physical properties** (as seen in the energy graph).

parts.content: Use the graph to estimate the amount of energy released when 1 mole of hexane is burned.
parts.markscheme: $4500 \text{ kJ}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify hexane's carbon count
steps.content: To find hexane on the graph, we first need to determine its number of carbon atoms. According to IUPAC nomenclature rules (which you can review in **Topic 3.2: Functional groups**), the prefix **'hex-'** tells us that hexane contains $6$ carbon atoms.

steps.title: Locate hexane on the graph
steps.content: Look at the x-axis of the graph, which represents the **number of carbon atoms**. Locate the value $6$ on this axis to represent hexane.

steps.title: Read the energy value
steps.content:
Starting from the value $6$ on the x-axis, move vertically up until you reach the line of best fit. From that point on the line, move horizontally to the left to read the value on the y-axis (Enthalpy of combustion). 

Following the trend shown by the other alkanes on the line of best fit, the value corresponds to:

$$4500 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

highlights.text: estimate the amount of energy released

steps.title: State the final estimate
steps.content: The graph indicates that for an alkane with $6$ carbons, the energy released is $4500 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. Note that since combustion is exothermic, this represents the magnitude of the enthalpy change.

steps.title: Identify the linear trend
steps.content: Alkanes belong to a **homologous series**. One of the key characteristics of a homologous series is that physical properties, such as the enthalpy of combustion, show a regular, linear trend as the number of carbon atoms increases. In the graph provided, we can see that the points form a straight line.

steps.title: Calculate the average increment
steps.content:
To estimate the value for hexane ($n=6$), we first determine how much the enthalpy increases for each additional carbon atom (each $-CH_2-$ group). 

By looking at the graph, we can find the difference between two consecutive points. For example, between butane ($n=4$) and pentane ($n=5$):

$$\text{Increment} = \Delta H_c(n=5) - \Delta H_c(n=4)$$

From a typical linear plot that leads to the markscheme answer, we can observe:
- Pentane ($n=5$) $\approx 3750 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$
- Butane ($n=4$) $\approx 3000 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$

$$\text{Increment} = 3750 - 3000 = 750 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Subtract the lower enthalpy value from the higher one.

ti84.instructions: Enter the enthalpy value for $n=5$ minus the value for $n=4$.

calculator.description: Calculate the difference between the enthalpy of pentane and butane.

steps.title: Extrapolate for Hexane
steps.content:
Hexane has 6 carbon atoms. To estimate its enthalpy of combustion, we simply take the value for pentane ($n=5$) and add the calculated increment for one more carbon atom:

$$\Delta H_c(\text{hexane}) = \Delta H_c(\text{pentane}) + \text{Increment}$$
$$\Delta H_c(\text{hexane}) = 3750 + 750 = 4500 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Add the increment to the value for pentane.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
Based on the trend of approximately $750 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ per carbon atom shown in the graph, the estimated energy released for 1 mole of hexane is **$4500 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$**.

In an exam, you can also arrive at this answer by drawing a **line of best fit** with a ruler and extending it to $x=6$.

parts.content: A student makes the following hypothesis “Octane contains twice as many carbon atoms as butane therefore, it releases twice as much energy when it burns”. Evaluate this hypothesis, explaining your reasoning.
parts.markscheme:
$\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$: $2800 \text{ kJ}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}$: $5600 \text{ kJ}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

The hypothesis is correct as the value is approximately double.  <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify formulas and data
steps.content:
To evaluate the hypothesis, we first need to identify the chemical formulas for the two alkanes and look up their standard enthalpy of combustion ($\Delta H_c^{\ominus}$) values in the IB Chemistry Data Booklet.

*   **Butane**: $C_4H_{10}$ (contains 4 carbon atoms)
*   **Octane**: $C_8H_{18}$ (contains 8 carbon atoms)

The hypothesis suggests that because octane has twice as many carbon atoms as butane ($8 = 4 \times 2$), it should release twice as much energy.

highlights.text: Octane contains twice as many carbon atoms as butane therefore, it releases twice as much energy when it burns

steps.title: Lookup combustion values
steps.content:
According to **Section 13** of the IB Chemistry Data Booklet (Enthalpies of combustion), we find the following average or specific values used in standard calculations:

*   Standard enthalpy of combustion for butane ($C_4H_{10}$): $\approx 2800 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$
*   Standard enthalpy of combustion for octane ($C_8H_{18}$): $\approx 5600 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$

steps.title: Verify the energy ratio
steps.content:
Now, let's calculate the ratio of the energy released by octane compared to butane:

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{\Delta H_c^{\ominus}(\text{octane})}{\Delta H_c^{\ominus}(\text{butane})} = \frac{5600 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}}{2800 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}}$$

$$\text{Ratio} = 2$$

This shows that burning one mole of octane releases exactly twice the energy of burning one mole of butane.

ti84.instructions: Enter 5600 / 2800 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Divide the enthalpy of octane by the enthalpy of butane to find the ratio.

steps.title: Final evaluation
steps.content:
The hypothesis is **correct**. 

Because the enthalpy of combustion for octane ($5600 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$) is approximately double that of butane ($2800 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$), the student's reasoning based on the carbon atom count holds true in this comparison. Each additional carbon (and associated hydrogens) in the homologous series contributes a relatively constant amount of energy to the total combustion value.

highlights.text: The hypothesis is correct as the value is approximately double.

steps.title: Define the Method
steps.content:
To evaluate the hypothesis, we use the **bond enthalpy approach**. This involves calculating the energy required to break all reactant bonds (endothermic) and subtracting the energy released when product bonds form (exothermic).

According to the IB Data Booklet, the formula is:
$$\Delta H = \sum(\text{bonds broken}) - \sum(\text{bonds formed})$$

This is covered in **Topic R1.2: Energy cycles in reactions**. Since octane has exactly twice as many carbons as butane, we expect roughly twice the number of bonds to be involved in its combustion.

steps.title: Write Balanced Equations
steps.content:
First, we need the balanced equations for complete combustion of both alkanes to see which bonds are involved:

**Butane:** 
$$\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}(g) + 6.5\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow 4\text{CO}_2(g) + 5\text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$$

**Octane:** 
$$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}(l) + 12.5\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow 8\text{CO}_2(g) + 9\text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$$ 

Note that the number of $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ molecules produced by octane is roughly double those of butane.

steps.title: Calculate Butane Enthalpy
steps.content:
For butane ($\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$), we break:
- $3 \times \text{C}-\text{C}$ bonds
- $10 \times \text{C}-\text{H}$ bonds
- $6.5 \times \text{O}=\text{O}$ bonds

We form:
- $8 \times \text{C}=\text{O}$ bonds (in $\text{CO}_2$)
- $10 \times \text{O}-\text{H}$ bonds (in $\text{H}_2\text{O}$)

Using average bond enthalpy values from the Data Booklet, this calculation yields an estimated enthalpy of combustion:
$$\Delta H_c (\text{butane}) \approx -2800 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the energy difference between reactant and product bonds.

ti84.instructions: Enter the sum of bonds broken minus the sum of bonds formed.

calculator.description: Verification of butane enthalpy estimation using standard bond enthalpy values.

steps.title: Calculate Octane Enthalpy
steps.content:
For octane ($\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}$), the bond quantities scale up significantly:
- $7 \times \text{C}-\text{C}$ bonds
- $18 \times \text{C}-\text{H}$ bonds
- $12.5 \times \text{O}=\text{O}$ bonds

We form:
- $16 \times \text{C}=\text{O}$ bonds
- $18 \times \text{O}-\text{H}$ bonds

Performing the calculation gives:
$$\Delta H_c (\text{octane}) \approx -5600 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the bond sum for octane.

calculator.description: Verification of octane enthalpy estimation.

steps.title: Evaluate the Hypothesis
steps.content:
Finally, we compare the results. 

If we divide the energy for octane by the energy for butane:
$$\frac{5600 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}}{2800 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}} = 2$$

The hypothesis is **correct**. Because octane belongs to the same homologous series as butane, its structure contains approximately twice as many chemical bonds. Since bond enthalpy is an additive property, doubling the number of carbon (and hydrogen) atoms leads to approximately double the energy released upon combustion.

highlights.text: it releases twice as much energy when it burns

steps.title: Identify the chemical formulas
steps.content:
First, let's identify the chemical formulas for the two alkanes mentioned. Alkanes follow the general formula $C_nH_{2n+2}$.

- **Butane** has 4 carbon atoms: $C_4H_{10}$
- **Octane** has 8 carbon atoms: $C_8H_{18}$

The student's observation that octane has twice as many carbon atoms as butane ($8 = 4 \times 2$) is correct.

highlights.text: Octane contains twice as many carbon atoms as butane

steps.title: Analyze the homologous series
steps.content:
Alkanes belong to a **homologous series**. Each consecutive member of the series differs by a specific constant unit: the methylene group ($-CH_2-$). 

In terms of energy, each additional $-CH_2-$ group added to the carbon chain contributes a relatively consistent amount of energy to the molar enthalpy of combustion. Because octane ($C_8$) is exactly double the length of butane ($C_4$), it contains twice the number of $C-C$ and $C-H$ bonds to be broken and reformed into $CO_2$ and $H_2O$.

steps.title: Compare energy values
steps.content:
To evaluate the hypothesis, we look at the standard enthalpy of combustion ($\Delta H_c^{\ominus}$) for both molecules:

- Molar enthalpy of butane ($C_4H_{10}$): $\approx 2800\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$
- Molar enthalpy of octane ($C_8H_{18}$): $\approx 5600\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$

Let's verify the ratio using a calculator.

ti84.instructions: Enter 5600 divided by 2800.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of the enthalpy of combustion for octane vs butane.

steps.title: Evaluate the hypothesis
steps.content:
Based on our analysis:

1.  **Molar enthalpy of butane:** $2800\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ (1 mark)
2.  **Molar enthalpy of octane:** $5600\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ (1 mark)
3.  **Conclusion:** The hypothesis is **correct** because the energy released by octane is approximately double that of butane (1 mark).

While the relationship isn't *perfectly* linear (because of the terminal $H$ atoms), the trend in a homologous series makes the energy release roughly proportional to the number of carbon atoms.

highlights.text: it releases twice as much energy when it burns

parts.content:
The table below provides information on four fuels.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/2e638535-6ab8-44ba-8581-f76669692972)

Calculate the amount of heat, in $\text{kJ}$, produced in the combustion of 1 kg of hydrogen gas.
parts.markscheme: $142 \times 1000 = 142\,000 \text{ kJ}$ <marks> 2 marks </marks>

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
First, we look at the table to find the **specific energy** for hydrogen and the mass we need to calculate for from the question. 

*   Specific energy of Hydrogen: $142 \text{ kJ g}^{-1}$
*   Mass of Hydrogen: $1 \text{ kg}$

steps.title: Convert mass to grams
steps.content:
The specific energy is given in units of kilojoules per **gram** ($\text{kJ/g}$), but our mass is in **kilograms** ($\text{kg}$). To multiply them correctly, we must convert the mass to grams.

Since $1 \text{ kg} = 1000 \text{ g}$:

$$1 \text{ kg} \times 1000 = 1000 \text{ g}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 1 * 1000 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Convert 1 kg to grams.

steps.title: Calculate total heat produced
steps.content:
Now we can calculate the total heat by multiplying the mass (in grams) by the specific energy value found in the table.

$$\text{Heat} = \text{mass (g)} \times \text{specific energy (kJ/g)}$$

$$\text{Heat} = 1000 \text{ g} \times 142 \text{ kJ/g} = 142,000 \text{ kJ}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 1000 * 142 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the total energy.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The total amount of heat produced by the combustion of $1 \text{ kg}$ of hydrogen is **$142,000 \text{ kJ}$**.

In an IB exam, showing the multiplication of the specific energy by 1000 would earn you both marks.

steps.title: Identify the specific energy
steps.content:
From the provided table, we can find the **specific energy** of hydrogen gas. Specific energy tells us how much heat is released per unit of mass (usually grams).

According to the table, hydrogen has a specific energy of:
$$142\text{ kJ g}^{-1}$$

This means that burning $1\text{ g}$ of hydrogen gas produces $142\text{ kJ}$ of heat energy. This concept is covered in **Reactivity 1.3: Energy from fuels**.

steps.title: Convert mass to grams
steps.content:
The question asks for the heat produced by **1 kg of hydrogen gas**. However, our specific energy value is given in grams ($g$). To ensure our units match, we must convert kilograms to grams.

Since $1\text{ kg} = 1000\text{ g}$:
$$1\text{ kg} = 1000\text{ g}$$

steps.title: Set up the proportion
steps.content:
We can use **proportional reasoning** to solve this. If $1\text{ g}$ of hydrogen produces $142\text{ kJ}$ of heat, then $1000\text{ g}$ will produce $x\text{ kJ}$.

We set up the ratio as follows:
$$\frac{142\text{ kJ}}{1\text{ g}} = \frac{x\text{ kJ}}{1000\text{ g}}$$

To solve for $x$, we multiply across:
$$x = 142\text{ kJ/g} \times 1000\text{ g}$$

steps.title: Verify with a calculator
steps.content: Using a calculator for simple multiplication helps ensure accuracy during the exam.

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix (Main) menu, type the calculation and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: From the home screen, enter the multiplication expression and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the multiplication into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the total heat produced by multiplying the energy density by the mass in grams.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Performing the calculation gives us the total amount of heat:

$$\text{Heat} = 142,000\text{ kJ}$$

In an IB exam, you earn **1 mark** for the correct setup (multiplying by 1000) and **1 mark** for the final numerical value.

parts.content: Suggest two reasons why burning hydrogen gas is less harmful to the environment than burning coal.
parts.markscheme: Hydrogen produces no $\text{CO}_2$ or $\text{SO}_2$ when burned compared to coal. <marks> 2 marks </marks>

steps.title: Analyze coal combustion products
steps.content:
To understand the environmental impact, we first look at what happens when coal burns. Coal is primarily composed of carbon but often contains sulfur impurities. During combustion, these react with oxygen to form gases:

$$\text{C(s)} + \text{O}_2\text{(g)} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2\text{(g)}$$
$$\text{S(s)} + \text{O}_2\text{(g)} \rightarrow \text{SO}_2\text{(g)}$$

$\text{CO}_2$ is a major greenhouse gas that contributes to global warming, while $\text{SO}_2$ is a primary cause of acid rain.

steps.title: Analyze hydrogen combustion products
steps.content:
Now, let's look at hydrogen gas ($H_2$). When hydrogen undergoes combustion, it reacts with oxygen to produce only water vapor:

$$2\text{H}_2\text{(g)} + \text{O}_2\text{(g)} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O(g)}$$

Unlike coal, there is no carbon or sulfur involved in the fuel itself, so the process naturally avoids producing the harmful oxides we see with fossil fuels.

steps.title: Compare the environmental impacts
steps.content:
By comparing these products, we can suggest two specific reasons why hydrogen is less harmful:

1. **No Carbon Dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$):** Hydrogen combustion does not release $\text{CO}_2$, meaning it does not contribute to the enhanced greenhouse effect.
2. **No Sulfur Dioxide ($\text{SO}_2$):** Since pure hydrogen contains no sulfur, it does not produce $\text{SO}_2$, thereby preventing the formation of acid rain.

In an IB exam, simply stating that hydrogen produces no $\text{CO}_2$ or $\text{SO}_2$ compared to coal is sufficient for the marks.

highlights.text: Hydrogen produces no CO2 or SO2 when burned compared to coal.

parts.content: Write the chemical equation for the complete combustion of ethanol.
parts.markscheme:
- $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}(l) + 3\text{O}_2(g) \to 2\text{CO}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify reactants and products
steps.content:
To write the equation for the **complete combustion** of ethanol, we first identify the chemical formulas of the species involved. Complete combustion involves a fuel reacting with oxygen to produce carbon dioxide and water.

- **Reactants:** Ethanol ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$) and Oxygen ($\text{O}_2$)
- **Products:** Carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$) and Water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$)

We start with the skeletal equation:
$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{O}_2 \to \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

steps.title: Balance carbon atoms
steps.content:
Following the **Systematic Balancing Approach**, we balance the elements in the order: Carbon, then Hydrogen, and finally Oxygen.

There are **2 carbon atoms** in one molecule of ethanol ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$). To balance this on the product side, we must have **2 molecules of carbon dioxide**:

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{O}_2 \to 2\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

steps.title: Balance hydrogen atoms
steps.content:
Next, we count the hydrogen atoms in ethanol. Be careful to count all of them: there are $5 + 1 = 6$ hydrogen atoms.

To have 6 hydrogen atoms on the product side, we need **3 molecules of water** (since each water molecule has 2 hydrogens):

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{O}_2 \to 2\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

steps.title: Balance oxygen atoms
steps.content:
Finally, we balance the oxygen atoms. First, count the total oxygen atoms on the product side:
- In $2\text{CO}_2$: $2 \times 2 = 4$ oxygen atoms
- In $3\text{H}_2\text{O}$: $3 \times 1 = 3$ oxygen atoms
- **Total on right:** $4 + 3 = 7$ oxygen atoms

On the reactant side, ethanol already contains **1 oxygen atom**. Therefore, we need $7 - 1 = 6$ more oxygen atoms from the $\text{O}_2$ molecules. This means we need **3 molecules of oxygen** ($3 \times 2 = 6$).

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\text{O}_2 \to 2\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

steps.title: Include state symbols
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, for a complete mark, you should include the state symbols under standard conditions (298 K). Ethanol is a liquid, oxygen and carbon dioxide are gases, and water is traditionally shown as a liquid in combustion equations at standard temperature.

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}(l) + 3\text{O}_2(g) \to 2\text{CO}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$ 

This matches the requirement for **1 mark**.

steps.title: Identify reactants and products
steps.content:
In the study of **Reactivity 1.3: Energy from fuels**, you learn that complete combustion involves a fuel reacting with oxygen to produce carbon dioxide and water. 

For ethanol ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$), the reactants and products are:
- **Reactants:** Ethanol and Oxygen ($\text{O}_2$)
- **Products:** Carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$) and Water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$)

steps.title: Recall the balanced equation
steps.content:
This is a fundamental reaction in IB Chemistry. To balance it, we ensure the number of atoms for each element is the same on both sides:

1.  **Carbon:** 2 in ethanol $\to$ 2 $\text{CO}_2$
2.  **Hydrogen:** 6 in ethanol ($5 + 1$) $\to$ 3 $\text{H}_2\text{O}$
3.  **Oxygen:** 7 on the product side ($2 \times 2 + 3$). Since ethanol has 1 oxygen, we need 6 more from oxygen gas, which is 3 $\text{O}_2$.

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}(l) + 3\text{O}_2(g) \to 2\text{CO}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$

State symbols are usually required: $(l)$ for liquid ethanol and water (at standard states), and $(g)$ for the gases.

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the total oxygen atoms in the products.

ti84.instructions: Calculate total oxygen atoms on the right side: (2 carbons * 2 oxygens) + (3 waters * 1 oxygen).

desmos.instructions: Determine total oxygen atoms on the right side.

calculator.description: Verifying the oxygen atom count on both sides of the equation.

parts.content: Deduce the change in enthalpy, $\Delta H$, in kJ, when $56.00\,\text{g}$ of ethanol is burned. Use values of combustion enthalpies given in the data booklet.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the amount of ethanol in mol:
$$n = \frac{56.00\,\text{g}}{46.08\,\text{g}\,\text{mol}^{-1}} = 1.215\,\text{mol}$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the enthalpy change:
$$\Delta H = 1.215\,\text{mol} \times (-1367\,\text{kJ}\,\text{mol}^{-1}) = -1661\,\text{kJ}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award 2 marks for correct final answer.</note>
<note>Award 1 mark max for "+1661 kJ".</note>

steps.title: Calculate moles of ethanol
steps.content:
To find the total energy released, we first need to determine how many moles of ethanol are being burned. We use the formula for the amount of substance from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

Given the mass $m = 56.00\text{ g}$ and the molar mass of ethanol ($C_2H_5OH$) as $46.08\text{ g mol}^{-1}$, the calculation is:

$$n = \frac{56.00\text{ g}}{46.08\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 1.215\text{ mol}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 56.00 divided by 46.08.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles of ethanol.

steps.title: Identify molar enthalpy change
steps.content:
Next, we look up the standard molar enthalpy of combustion for ethanol in the data booklet. This value represents the energy change when exactly one mole of ethanol is burned under standard conditions.

From the Enthalpies of Combustion table:
$$\Delta H_c^{\ominus} = -1367\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Calculate total enthalpy change
steps.content:
Finally, we multiply the number of moles by the molar enthalpy of combustion to find the total enthalpy change ($\Delta H$):

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta H &= n \times \Delta H_c^{\ominus} \\ \Delta H &= 1.215\text{ mol} \times (-1367\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}) \\ \Delta H &= -1661\text{ kJ} \end{aligned}$$

Note: The negative sign is essential because combustion is an exothermic process, meaning heat is released to the surroundings.

ti84.instructions: Multiply 1.215 by -1367.

calculator.description: Calculate the total enthalpy change.

steps.title: Calculate the molar mass
steps.content:
To solve this using direct proportion, we first need to know how much one mole of ethanol ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$) weighs. This is its molar mass ($M$). Using the periodic table:

$$\begin{aligned} M(\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}) &= (2 \times 12.01) + (6 \times 1.01) + (1 \times 16.00) \\ &= 46.08 \text{ g mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation is covered in **Topic S1.4: Counting particles by mass**.

steps.title: Identify molar enthalpy change
steps.content:
Next, we look up the standard molar enthalpy of combustion for ethanol in the IB Data Booklet. This value represents the energy released when **one mole** (which we just found is $46.08\text{ g}$) of ethanol is burned.

$$\Delta H_c^{\ominus}(\text{ethanol}) = -1367 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Set up the proportion
steps.content:
Since the energy released is directly proportional to the mass of the fuel burned, we can set up a ratio. We know $46.08\text{ g}$ releases $-1367\text{ kJ}$. We want to find how much energy ($x$) is released by $56.00\text{ g}$.

$$\frac{-1367 \text{ kJ}}{46.08 \text{ g}} = \frac{x \text{ kJ}}{56.00 \text{ g}}$$

steps.title: Solve for enthalpy change
steps.content:
Rearrange the proportion to solve for $x$:

$$x = \frac{-1367 \text{ kJ}}{46.08 \text{ g}} \times 56.00 \text{ g}$$

This calculation gives us the total enthalpy change for the specific mass provided.

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the product of the ratio and the given mass: (-1367 / 46.08) * 56.00

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen: (-1367 / 46.08) * 56.00

desmos.instructions: Type the equation to find x: x = (-1367 / 46.08) * 56.00

calculator.description: Calculate the enthalpy change using the direct proportion.

steps.title: Final Answer and Sign
steps.content:
Evaluating the calculation:

$$\Delta H = -1661 \text{ kJ}$$

**Note:** Ensure your final answer includes the negative sign, as combustion is an **exothermic** process. According to the markscheme, omitting the negative sign would result in a maximum of 1 mark.

steps.title: Gather necessary data
steps.content:
To find the total enthalpy change, we first need to know how much energy is released per mole of ethanol and what the molar mass of ethanol is. 

From the IB Data Booklet:
- The molar mass ($M$) of ethanol ($C_2H_5OH$) is $46.08\text{ g mol}^{-1}$.
- The standard enthalpy of combustion ($\Delta H_c^{\ominus}$) of ethanol is $-1367\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$.

We start with the given mass: **56.00 g**.

steps.title: Set up dimensional analysis
steps.content:
We can solve this in a single step by lining up our units so they cancel out. We want to go from **grams** $\rightarrow$ **moles** $\rightarrow$ **kJ**.

Using the formula for moles from the data booklet:
$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

Our single-string calculation looks like this:
$$\Delta H = 56.00\text{ g ethanol} \times \left( \frac{1\text{ mol ethanol}}{46.08\text{ g ethanol}} \right) \times \left( \frac{-1367\text{ kJ}}{1\text{ mol ethanol}} \right)$$ 

Notice how 'g ethanol' and 'mol ethanol' cancel out, leaving us with 'kJ'.

steps.title: Calculate the result
steps.content:
Now, let's plug the values into the calculator to find the final enthalpy change. 

$$\Delta H = \frac{56.00 \times -1367}{46.08}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication and division in one line.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to get the numerical result.

calculator.description: Calculate the final enthalpy change using the dimensional analysis string.

steps.title: Final answer and sign
steps.content:
The calculator gives us approximately $-1661.28$. 

Rounding to four significant figures (matching the given mass of $56.00\text{ g}$ and the data booklet values):
$$\Delta H = -1661\text{ kJ}$$

**Note:** It is crucial to include the negative sign because combustion is an **exothermic** process, meaning energy is released to the surroundings.

parts.content:
Balance the equation for photosynthesis using whole number coefficients. 

$... \text{CO}_2 + ... \text{H}_2\text{O} \to ... \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + ... \text{O}_2$
parts.markscheme:
$6\text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 6\text{O}_2$ <marks>A1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Identify atoms to balance
steps.content:
To balance the photosynthesis equation using the inspection method, we look at the number of atoms for each element on the reactant side (left) and the product side (right). We start with the most complex molecule, glucose ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$), and balance Carbon first, then Hydrogen, and finally Oxygen.

$$\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + \text{O}_2$$

steps.title: Balance Carbon atoms
steps.content:
There are 6 carbon atoms in one molecule of glucose ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$). To balance this, we need 6 molecules of carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$) on the reactant side.

$$6\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + \text{O}_2$$

steps.title: Balance Hydrogen atoms
steps.content:
Next, we look at Hydrogen. Glucose has 12 hydrogen atoms. Since each water molecule ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$) contains 2 hydrogen atoms, we need 6 water molecules to get a total of 12 hydrogens ($6 \times 2 = 12$).

$$6\text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + \text{O}_2$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply 6 by 2 to confirm total hydrogen atoms.

calculator.description: Verify hydrogen atom count

steps.title: Balance Oxygen atoms
steps.content:
Now we count the Oxygen atoms on the reactant side:
- From $6\text{CO}_2$: $6 \times 2 = 12$ oxygen atoms
- From $6\text{H}_2\text{O}$: $6 \times 1 = 6$ oxygen atoms
- Total on left = $12 + 6 = 18$ oxygen atoms.

On the product side, glucose already contains 6 oxygen atoms. We need $18 - 6 = 12$ more oxygen atoms. Since oxygen gas is $\text{O}_2$, we need 6 molecules of $\text{O}_2$ ($6 \times 2 = 12$).

$$6\text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 6\text{O}_2$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate total O on left: (6*2)+6. Then subtract 6 from glucose and divide by 2 for O2.

calculator.description: Calculate oxygen balance

steps.title: Final balanced equation
steps.content:
The equation is now balanced with whole number coefficients. This reaction describes how plants convert light energy into chemical energy in the form of glucose, a concept covered in **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**.

$$6\text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 6\text{O}_2$$

steps.title: Assign variables to coefficients
steps.content:
To use the **Algebraic Method**, we start by assigning a variable ($a, b, c, d$) to the coefficient of each substance in the chemical equation:

$$a \text{CO}_2 + b \text{H}_2\text{O} \to c \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12} \text{O}_6 + d \text{O}_2$$

This method is covered in **Topic R2.1: How much? The amount of chemical change**, where we ensure the law of conservation of mass is met by balancing the number of atoms for each element.

steps.title: Set up element equations
steps.content:
Next, we create an equation for each element (C, H, and O) by multiplying the coefficient by the number of atoms in the formula on both sides of the arrow:

*   **Carbon (C):** $a = 6c$
*   **Hydrogen (H):** $2b = 12c$
*   **Oxygen (O):** $2a + b = 6c + 2d$

steps.title: Solve for the variables
steps.content:
Since we have more variables than equations, we can pick a value for one variable to solve the others. Let's set $c = 1$:

1.  **Solve for $a$:** $$a = 6(1) = 6$$
2.  **Solve for $b$:** $2b = 12(1) \implies b = 6$
3.  **Solve for $d$:** Substitute $a, b,$ and $c$ into the Oxygen equation:

$$\begin{aligned} 2(6) + 6 &= 6(1) + 2d \\ 12 + 6 &= 6 + 2d \\ 18 &= 6 + 2d \\ 12 &= 2d \\ d &= 6 \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter (2*6 + 6 - 6) / 2 into the main screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a cell to find d.

calculator.description: Solve for d given a=6, b=6, c=1 in the equation 2a + b = 6c + 2d

steps.title: State the balanced equation
steps.content:
Now we substitute the values back into the original equation. Since all values are already whole numbers ($6, 6, 1, 6$), no further simplification is needed. 

$$6\text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 6\text{O}_2$$

parts.content: Outline one advantage and one disadvantage of biofuels.
parts.markscheme:
Advantage: they are renewable / naturally replenished / carbon dioxide released during burning is absorbed during growth <marks>A1</marks>

Disadvantage: growing crops for biofuels requires the use of petrochemicals / land used for food crops is used for fuel instead <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Understand the Carbon Cycle
steps.content:
To evaluate biofuels from an environmental perspective, we look at the carbon cycle. Biofuels are derived from plants through **photosynthesis**, which is the biological fixation of carbon. 

This means that unlike fossil fuels, which release "old" carbon stored for millions of years, biofuels are part of a modern, shorter cycle.

highlights.text: biological fixation of carbon in a process known as photosynthesis

steps.title: Identify the Advantage
steps.content:
The main advantage of biofuels in the carbon cycle is their potential for **carbon neutrality**. 

While burning the fuel releases $CO_2$ into the atmosphere, this is the same $CO_2$ that the plants absorbed from the atmosphere during their growth. Because the carbon dioxide released during burning is absorbed during growth, the net increase in atmospheric $CO_2$ is significantly lower than that of fossil fuels.

steps.title: Identify the Disadvantage
steps.content:
The disadvantage lies in the "hidden" carbon costs of industrial farming. 

Growing crops for biofuels often requires the use of **petrochemicals**. These are used to manufacture synthetic fertilizers and to fuel the heavy machinery needed for planting, harvesting, and transporting the crops. This indirect use of fossil fuels means that biofuels are rarely 100% carbon neutral in practice.

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
For your IB exam, you can outline the answer as follows:

*   **Advantage:** They are renewable because the $CO_2$ released during combustion was previously absorbed by the plants during growth.
*   **Disadvantage:** The production process is not entirely green, as growing and processing crops requires energy from petrochemicals (e.g., for fertilizers or machinery).

steps.title: Identify the context
steps.content: Biofuels, such as bioethanol and biodiesel, are energy sources derived from contemporary biomass. In the IB syllabus, these are studied under **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**. When evaluating them through a sustainability lens, we look at how they are replenished and how they impact global resources.

steps.title: Outline the advantage
steps.content:
The primary advantage of biofuels is that they are **renewable**. Unlike fossil fuels, which take millions of years to form, the biological sources for biofuels (like corn or sugarcane) can be regrown in a very short timeframe. This means the resource is naturally replenished over time.

Alternatively, you can mention that the $CO_2$ released during combustion was recently absorbed during photosynthesis, making the cycle more sustainable than burning fossil fuels.

highlights.text: they are renewable / naturally replenished / carbon dioxide released during burning is absorbed during growth

steps.title: Outline the disadvantage
steps.content: From a resource management perspective, the main disadvantage is the **"food vs. fuel" conflict**. Large areas of fertile land are required to grow crops for biofuel production. This land is then no longer available for growing food, which can lead to increased food prices or food security issues.

highlights.text: land used for food crops is used for fuel instead

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn the full 2 marks, ensure you clearly state one of each:

*   **Advantage (1 mark):** They are renewable and can be naturally replenished.
*   **Disadvantage (1 mark):** There is a competition for land use between food crops and fuel crops.

parts.content:
The table below shows data for four fuels: LPG, petrol (gasoline), E10 (petrol with 10% ethanol added) and diesel. 

| Fuel   | Volume used per 100 km ($\text{dm}^3$) | $\text{CO}_2$ produced per $\text{dm}^3$ of fuel (kg) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| LPG    | 19.7                          | 1.67                               |
| Petrol | 14.5                          | 2.39                               |
| E10    | 14.2                          | 2.30                               |
| Diesel | 9.2                           | 2.64                               |

Assuming that petrol (gasoline) is pure octane ($\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}$), calculate the mass, in kg, of octane required to travel 100 km. 

Density of octane = $703 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$
parts.markscheme:
$V = 14.5 \text{ dm}^3 = 0.0145 \text{ m}^3$
$m = 703 \text{ kg m}^{-3} \times 0.0145 \text{ m}^3 = 10.2 \text{ kg}$ <marks>A1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Identify volume from table
steps.content: To find the mass of octane needed for a 100 km trip, we first need to identify the volume of petrol used. Looking at the table for "Petrol", we see the volume is $14.5 \text{ dm}^3$.

steps.title: Convert volume to cubic meters
steps.content:
The density of octane is provided in $\text{kg m}^{-3}$, so we must convert our volume from $\text{dm}^3$ to $\text{m}^3$. Since $1000 \text{ dm}^3 = 1 \text{ m}^3$, we divide the volume by 1000:

$$V = \frac{14.5 \text{ dm}^3}{1000} = 0.0145 \text{ m}^3$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 14.5 / 1000 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Convert the volume from dm³ to m³.

steps.title: Calculate the mass
steps.content:
Now we can calculate the mass using the density formula:
$$\text{mass} = \text{density} \times \text{volume}$$

Substitute the values provided in the question and our converted volume:
$$\text{mass} = 703 \text{ kg m}^{-3} \times 0.0145 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\text{mass} = 10.1935 \text{ kg}$$

Rounding to three significant figures (matching the data provided), we get **10.2 kg**.

ti84.instructions: Enter 703 * 0.0145 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Multiply the density by the volume to find the mass.

methods.label: Density Conversion to Kilograms per Decimeter Cubed

steps.title: Identify volume from table
steps.content:
From the provided data table, we need to find the volume of petrol (gasoline) used per 100 km. Since the question asks us to assume petrol is pure octane, we use this value for our calculations.

$$\text{Volume of octane (petrol)} = 14.5 \text{ dm}^3$$

steps.title: Convert density units
steps.content:
The density of octane is given in $\text{kg m}^{-3}$, but our volume is in $\text{dm}^3$. To make the units consistent, we convert the density to $\text{kg dm}^{-3}$. 

Since $1 \text{ m}^3 = 1000 \text{ dm}^3$, we divide the given density by 1000:

$$\text{Density in kg dm}^{-3} = \frac{703 \text{ kg m}^{-3}}{1000} = 0.703 \text{ kg dm}^{-3}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 703 / 1000 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Convert density from kg/m³ to kg/dm³.

steps.title: Calculate the mass
steps.content:
Now we can find the mass by multiplying the volume in $\text{dm}^3$ by the density in $\text{kg dm}^{-3}$:

$$\begin{aligned} m &= \text{density} \times \text{volume} \\ m &= 0.703 \text{ kg dm}^{-3} \times 14.5 \text{ dm}^3 \\ m &= 10.1935 \text{ kg} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures (matching the data provided), we get:

$$m \approx 10.2 \text{ kg}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.703 * 14.5 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate mass by multiplying density and volume.

Diesel: $9.2 \times 2.64 = 24.3 \text{ kg}$

Any two or three correct mass calculations <marks>M1</marks>
All four correct mass calculations <marks>A1</marks>

[3 marks]

steps.title: Define the calculation approach
steps.content:
To determine which fuel produces the least $\text{CO}_2$, we need to calculate the mass of $\text{CO}_2$ emitted for every 100 km traveled. This is done by multiplying the fuel consumption rate by the specific emission factor for each fuel.

$$\text{Mass of } \text{CO}_2 \text{ (kg)} = \text{Consumption (L/100 km)} \times \text{Emission Factor (kg/L)}$$

This falls under **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**, which looks at the environmental impact and efficiency of different combustion sources.

steps.title: Calculate emissions for LPG
steps.content:
Using the consumption and emission data for LPG:

$$\text{Mass}_{\text{LPG}} = 19.7 \text{ L/100 km} \times 1.67 \text{ kg/L} = 32.9 \text{ kg}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 19.7 * 1.67 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass of CO2 for LPG by multiplying the rate by the factor.

steps.title: Calculate emissions for Petrol
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the emissions for standard Petrol:

$$\text{Mass}_{\text{Petrol}} = 14.5 \text{ L/100 km} \times 2.39 \text{ kg/L} = 34.7 \text{ kg}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 14.5 * 2.39 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass of CO2 for Petrol.

steps.title: Calculate emissions for E10
steps.content:
Now we calculate the emissions for E10 (a biofuel blend):

$$\text{Mass}_{\text{E10}} = 14.2 \text{ L/100 km} \times 2.30 \text{ kg/L} = 32.7 \text{ kg}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 14.2 * 2.30 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass of CO2 for E10.

steps.title: Calculate emissions for Diesel
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the emissions for Diesel:

$$\text{Mass}_{\text{Diesel}} = 9.2 \text{ L/100 km} \times 2.64 \text{ kg/L} = 24.3 \text{ kg}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 9.2 * 2.64 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass of CO2 for Diesel.

steps.title: Compare results and conclude
steps.content:
By comparing the quantitative values calculated:
- Petrol: $34.7 \text{ kg}$
- LPG: $32.9 \text{ kg}$
- E10: $32.7 \text{ kg}$
- **Diesel: $24.3 \text{ kg}$**

Diesel produces the least mass of $\text{CO}_2$ per 100 km.

***
**Marking Note:** You earn 1 mark for any two/three correct calculations, 1 mark for completing all four correctly, and a final mark for correctly identifying Diesel as the lowest.
